Check: 210° = 7/12 of a full circle. Circle area = π(25) = 25π. Sector = (7/12)(25π) = 175π/12. ✓

Tip for fraction × fraction: (25/2) × (7π/6) = (25×7π)/(2×6) = 175π/12.
questions.formula: Convert to radians first; then A = ½r²θ

questions.question_text: A sector has central angle 90° and radius 10 m. What is the arc length? (Convert degrees to radians first.)
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Convert 90° to radians.
90° × (π/180) = π/2 radians

Step 2: Apply s = θr.
s = (π/2) × 10 = 5π meters

Check: 90° = ¼ of a full circle. Circumference = 2π(10) = 20π. One-quarter = 5π. ✓

Trap watch: D) 25π — computing ½r²θ (area formula) instead of rθ (length formula). Always distinguish: LENGTH uses s=rθ (one factor of r); AREA uses A=½r²θ (two factors of r and a ½).
questions.hint: Convert 90° to π/2 radians first. Then s = θr = (π/2)(10). Or: 90° is ¼ of a circle, so arc = ¼ × 2πr.
questions.formula: Convert first: deg × π/180; then s = θr

questions.question_text: Two sectors share the same central angle of π/3 radians. Sector A has radius 3 m and Sector B has radius 6 m. What is the ratio of Sector B's area to Sector A's area?
questions.explanation:
Use the sector area formula to find each area, or use the ratio property.

Method 1 — Compute directly:
A_A = ½(3²)(π/3) = ½(9)(π/3) = 3π/2
A_B = ½(6²)(π/3) = ½(36)(π/3) = 6π

Ratio = A_B/A_A = 6π ÷ (3π/2) = 6π × (2/3π) = 4

Method 2 — Ratio shortcut (same θ):
Since A = ½r²θ and θ is the same for both:
A_B/A_A = r_B²/r_A² = 6²/3² = 36/9 = 4

For sectors with equal central angles, area scales as the SQUARE of the radius ratio.
questions.hint: Since θ is the same for both sectors, the ratio of areas = ratio of r². So (6/3)² = 4.
questions.formula: For equal θ: A_B/A_A = (r_B/r_A)² = r_B²/r_A²

questions.question_text: In a 3-hour examination of 320 questions, there are 40 mathematics problems. If twice as much time should be allowed for each mathematics problem as for each of the other questions, how many minutes should be spent on the mathematics problems?
questions.explanation: 3 hours = 180 minutes. Let each math question get x minutes; each other question gets x/2. 40x + 280(x/2)=180, so 180x=180 and x=1. Math time = 40 minutes.

What is the square root of 9 raised to the fourth power?

questions.explanation: When multiplying powers with the same base, add exponents: 3+5=8.

questions.explanation: 10^-2 moves the decimal two places left: 3.47 → 0.0347.

questions.explanation: 100,000 is 1 followed by 5 zeros, so it is 10^5.

questions.explanation:
Complementary angles add to 90°. 71°+19°=90°.

questions.question_text: If the measures of three angles in a triangle are in the ratio 2:6:10, what is the measure of the smallest angle?
questions.explanation: Triangle angles sum to 180. 2x+6x+10x=18x=180, so x=10. Smallest angle = 2x = 20.

questions.question_text: If a circle has a diameter of 12 cm, what is its area?
questions.explanation: Radius = 12/2=6. Area = πr² = 36π ≈ 113 cm².

questions.question_text: A rectangular solid measures 12 cm by 3 cm by 9 cm. What is its volume?
questions.explanation: Volume = length×width×height = 12×3×9 = 324 cm³.

questions.question_text: The perimeter of a square is 24 m. What is its area?
questions.explanation: Each side = 24/4=6. Area = 6×6=36 m².

questions.question_text: If the sum of the edges of a cube is 48 inches, the volume of the cube is
questions.explanation: A cube has 12 edges. Each edge = 48/12=4. Volume=4³=64 cubic inches.

questions.question_text: The hour hand of a clock is 3 feet long. How many feet does the tip of this hand move between 1:00 pm and 5:00 pm?
questions.explanation: The tip traces a circle of radius 3. Full circumference = 6π. Four hours is 4/12=1/3 of a full circle. Distance = 2π.

questions.question_text: Find the area of a square circumscribed about a circle whose radius is 10.
questions.explanation: A square circumscribed about the circle has side equal to the circle diameter, 20. Area=20×20=400.

questions.question_text: If a triangle of base 6 has the same area as a circle of radius 6, what is the altitude of the triangle?
questions.explanation: Circle area = π(6²)=36π. Triangle area = 1/2(6)h=3h. Set 3h=36π, so h=12π.

questions.question_text: In the figure, circle center O connects to points A and B on the circle. Chord AB is shown and the central angle AOB is 70°. What is the measure of angle x at point B?
questions.explanation: OA and OB are equal radii, so triangle AOB is isosceles. Base angles are equal: (180-70)/2=55°.

questions.question_text: Suppose the area of the square in the diagram is 64 in². A circle is inscribed in the square. What is the area of the circle?
questions.explanation: Square side = √64=8. The inscribed circle diameter is 8, so radius=4. Area=π(4²)=16π.

questions.question_text: The area of a square is 36 square inches. If the side of this square is doubled, the area of the new square will be
questions.explanation: Original side = √36=6. Doubled side =12. New area=12²=144, which is not listed.

questions.question_text: How many meters will a point on the rim of a wheel travel if the wheel makes 35 rotations and its radius is one meter?
questions.explanation: One rotation travels one circumference: 2πr=2π. Using π≈22/7, 35 rotations =35×2×22/7=220.

questions.question_text: The hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are 5 inches and 12 inches is
questions.explanation: 5-12-13 is a Pythagorean triple. Or c²=25+144=169, so c=13.

questions.question_text: The sum of the angle measures of a pentagon is
questions.explanation: Interior angle sum = (n-2)×180. For n=5: 3×180=540°.

questions.question_text: The volume of a cylinder with a radius of r and a height of h is
questions.explanation: Cylinder volume is πr²h, which is not one of choices A-D.

questions.question_text: On a six-sided die, each side has a number between 1 and 6. What is the probability of throwing a 3 or 4?
questions.explanation: There are 2 favorable outcomes, 3 or 4, out of 6 total outcomes. 2/6 = 1/3.

questions.question_text: When 550 gallons of oil are added to an oil tank that is 1/8 full, the tank becomes 1/2 full. The capacity of the oil tank is most nearly
questions.explanation: 550 gallons equals the difference between 1/2 and 1/8 of the tank: 3/8 capacity. Capacity = 550×8/3 ≈ 1467, closest 1470.

questions.explanation: From a=3b, 2a=6b. Since 6b=12c, then 2a=12c and a=6c.

If (x-y)² = 40 and x² + y² = 60, then xy =
questions.explanation: (x-y)²=x²+y²-2xy. Substitute: 40=60-2xy, so 2xy=20 and xy=10.

questions.question_text: Find the second of three consecutive integers if the sum of the first and third is 26.
questions.explanation: Let the integers be n-1, n, n+1. First+third = (n-1)+(n+1)=2n=26, so n=13.

questions.question_text: Simplify the following expression: (2x^4 y^7 m^2 z)(5x^2 y^3 m^8)
questions.explanation: Multiply coefficients and add exponents: 2×5=10, x^(4+2), y^(7+3), m^(2+8), and keep z.

questions.question_text: Simplify the following expression: (2x² + 3)(2x - 1)
questions.explanation: Distribute: 2x²(2x-1)+3(2x-1)=4x³-2x²+6x-3.

questions.question_text: A 6 foot-tall farmer wants to determine the height of his barn. He notices that his shadow is 10 feet long and his barn casts a shadow 75 feet long. How high is the barn?
questions.explanation: Use similar triangles: 6/10 = x/75, so 10x = 450 and x = 45 feet.

questions.question_text: Two planes left at the same time from two airports that are 6000 miles apart and flew toward each other. They passed each other in five hours. The rate of the fast plane was twice the rate of the slow plane. What was the speed of the fast plane?
questions.explanation: Let slow plane rate = x and fast plane = 2x. In 5 hours: 5x + 10x = 6000, so 15x=6000 and x=400. Fast plane = 800 mph.

questions.question_text: Harvey paid $400 for a used car that travels 28 miles per gallon on the highway and 20 miles per gallon in the city. If he drove twice as many highway miles as city miles last month while using 34 gallons of gasoline, how many miles did he drive altogether?
questions.explanation: Let city miles=x and highway miles=2x. Gallons: x/20 + 2x/28 =34. Multiply by 140: 7x+10x=4760, x=280. Total =3x=840.

questions.question_text: If an aircraft is traveling at 630 miles per hour, how many miles does it cover in 1200 seconds?
questions.explanation:
1200 seconds = 1200/3600 = 1/3 hour. Distance = 630×1/3 = 210 miles.

Source note: PDF answer key text appears mismatched from another problem; this item was corrected mathematically.

questions.question_text: It takes a runner 9 seconds to run a distance of 132 feet. What is the runner’s speed in miles per hour? (5280 ft = 1 mile)
If there are red, green, and yellow marbles in a jar and 20% of these marbles are either red or green, what are the chances of blindly picking a yellow marble out of the jar?
questions.explanation: If 20% are red or green, the remaining 80% are yellow. 80% = 4/5.

questions.question_text: Each corridor contains 8 to 10 classrooms and each classroom contains 20 to 24 students. If all classrooms are occupied, what is the minimum number of students on one corridor at a given time?
questions.explanation: Minimum uses the lowest values: 8 classrooms × 20 students = 160.

questions.question_text: A naval detachment has enough rations to feed sixteen people for 10 days. If four more people join the detachment, for how many fewer days will the rations last?
questions.explanation: Total person-days = 16×10 = 160. With 20 people, days = 160/20 = 8. That is 2 fewer days.

questions.question_text: A field can be plowed by 9 machines in 5 hours. If 3 machines are broken and cannot be used, how many hours will it take to plow the field?
questions.explanation: Machine-hours = 9×5 = 45. With 6 working machines, time = 45/6 = 7.5 hours.

questions.question_text: Nicholas receives a basic weekly salary of $180 plus a 5% commission. In a week in which his sales amounted to $1800, the ratio of his basic salary to his commission was
questions.explanation: Commission = 5% of 1800 = 90. Basic salary:commission = 180:90 = 2:1.

questions.question_text: The ratio of Democrats to Republicans in a certain state legislature is 5:7. If the legislature has 156 members, all of whom are either Democrats or Republicans, what is the difference between the number of Republicans and the number of Democrats?
questions.explanation: Total parts = 5+7=12. Each part = 156/12=13. Difference = 2 parts = 26.

questions.question_text: Successive discounts of 20% and 15% are equivalent to a single discount of
questions.explanation: Start with 100. After 20% off: 80. Then 15% off 80: 68. Total discount = 32%.

questions.question_text: In order to check on a shipment of 500 articles, a sampling of 50 articles was carefully inspected. Of the sample, 4 articles were found to be defective. On this basis, what is the probable percentage of defective articles in the original shipment?
questions.explanation: 4 defective out of 50 means 4/50 = 0.08 = 8%.

questions.question_text: The price of a one-hundred-dollar item after successive discounts of 10% and 15% is
questions.explanation: $100 after 10% off is $90. Then 15% off $90 is $13.50, so final price = $76.50.

questions.question_text: A certain governmental agency had a budget last year of $1,100,500. Its budget this year was 7% higher than that of last year. The budget for next year is 8% higher than this year’s budget. Which one of the following is the agency’s budget for next year?

questions.explanation: Area = length×width = 5×7 = 35 cm².

questions.question_text: If the volume of a cube is 8 cm³, what is the length of one side of the cube?
questions.explanation: Cube volume = side³. Since 2³=8, the side length is 2 cm.

questions.explanation: Area = 27×32=864 ft². Carpet width = 27 inches = 2.25 ft. Length needed = 864/2.25=384 ft = 128 yd.

questions.question_text: The floor area in a Navy warehouse measures 200 feet by 200 feet. What is the maximum safe floor load if the maximum weight the floor area can hold is 4000 tons?

questions.explanation: Equilateral perimeter = 3s. If s=5, perimeter=15, divisible by both 3 and 5.

questions.explanation: Square numerator and denominator: (2/5)^2 = 4/25.

questions.question_text: Mrs. Norton spent 2/3 of the family income one year and divided the remainder among 4 different savings accounts. If she puts $2000 into each account, what was the family income that year?
questions.explanation: Savings total = 4×2000=8000. This is the remaining 1/3 of income, so income = 8000×3 = 24000.

questions.question_text: Eight hundred persons are employed by the Metropolitan Transit Authority. One quarter are college graduates; 5/6 of the remainder are high school graduates. What part of the total number of employees never graduated from high school?
questions.explanation: College graduates = 1/4 of 800 =200, leaving 600. High school grads = 5/6 of 600=500. Non-high-school grads =100, and 100/800=1/8.

questions.question_text: A pole 63 feet long was broken into two unequal parts so that 3/5 of the longer piece equaled 3/4 of the shorter piece. Find the length of the longer piece.
questions.explanation: Let longer = x and shorter = 63-x. (3/5)x=(3/4)(63-x). Solving gives x=35.

questions.question_text: A purse contains $2.20 in dimes and quarters. If the number of dimes is 1/4 the number of quarters, how many dimes are there?
questions.explanation: Let dimes=x, quarters=4x. Value: 0.10x+0.25(4x)=2.20, so 1.10x=2.20 and x=2.

questions.question_text: If a is greater than 2, which of the following is the smallest?
questions.explanation: The expressions with a in the denominator are less than 1. Since a+1 is the largest denominator, 2/(a+1) is the smallest.

questions.question_text: The Spencers took t dollars in traveler’s checks with them on a trip. During the first week, they spent 1/5 of their money. During the second week, they spent 1/3 of the remainder. How much did they have left at the end of the second week?
questions.explanation: After week 1, left = 4/5 t. After spending 1/3 of the remainder, left = 2/3 of that: (2/3)(4/5)t = 8t/15.

questions.question_text: If r planes carry p passengers, how many planes are needed to carry m passengers?
questions.explanation: Planes per passenger = r/p. For m passengers, planes needed = (r/p)m = rm/p.

questions.question_text: If 16x + 4 = 100, what is the value of x?

questions.explanation:
One carton has 20×10=200 cigarettes. Total tar = 200×12=2400 mg = 2.4 g.

questions.question_text: One recruit can complete a certain assignment in 40 minutes; another recruit can complete the same assignment in one hour. How long would it take to complete the assignment if the two recruits worked together?
questions.explanation: Combined rate = 1/40 + 1/60 = 3/120 + 2/120 = 5/120 = 1/24 job/minute. Time = 24 minutes.

questions.question_text: A pole 24 feet high has a shadow 8 feet long. A nearby pole is 72 feet high. How long is its shadow?
questions.explanation: Use similar triangles: 24/8 = 72/x, so 3 = 72/x and x = 24 feet.

questions.question_text: Harvey paid $400 for a used car that travels 28 miles per gallon on the highway and 20 miles per gallon in the city. If he drove twice as many highway miles as city miles while using 34 gallons of gasoline, how many miles did he drive altogether?
questions.explanation: Let city miles = x and highway miles = 2x. Gallons used: x/20 + 2x/28 = 34. Multiply by 140: 7x+10x=4760, so x=280. Total miles = 3x = 840.

questions.explanation: Outer dimensions = (16+6) by (12+6) = 22×18=396. Bed area = 16×12=192. Walk area = 396-192=204.

questions.question_text: A team won 25 games in a 40-game season. Find the ratio of games won to games lost.
questions.explanation: Games lost = 40 - 25 = 15. Won:lost = 25:15 = 5:3.

questions.explanation: 7 adults for 2 days = 14 adult-days uses 15 cans. 4 adults for 7 days = 28 adult-days, double the amount, so 30 cans.

questions.question_text: The square root of 250 is between
questions.explanation: 15^2=225 and 16^2=256. Since 250 is between 225 and 256, sqrt(250) is between 15 and 16.

questions.question_text: What part of a dime is a quarter?
questions.explanation: A quarter is 25 cents and a dime is 10 cents. 25/10=5/2.

questions.question_text: At 12:00 noon, two vessels started sailing toward each other from ports 450 miles apart. They traveled at average rates of 22 and 28 mph, respectively. How many miles apart will the vessels be at 8 pm?
questions.explanation: In 8 hours they cover 22×8 + 28×8 =176+224=400 miles. Distance remaining =450-400=50.

questions.explanation: One stick is 1/4 lb. Four tablespoons is half a stick, so half of 1/4 lb = 1/8 lb.

questions.explanation: 1 inch on the drawing represents 24,000 inches actual. 24,000 inches = 2,000 feet.

questions.question_text: If the entrance requirement of a certain college is 82, what mark must a student have in Geometry (weight 2) if his other marks are English 88 (weight 3), Spanish 78 (weight 2), and History 80 (weight 2)?
questions.explanation: Total weight = 9, required points = 82×9=738. Known points = 88×3 +78×2 +80×2 =580. Geometry points needed=158, and 158/2=79.

questions.question_text: A crate containing a tool weighs 12 pounds. If the tool weighs 9 pounds, 9 ounces, how much does the crate weigh?
questions.explanation: Borrow 1 pound = 16 ounces: 12 lb 0 oz = 11 lb 16 oz. Subtract 9 lb 9 oz to get 2 lb 7 oz.

questions.question_text: Assume that it takes an average of 3 man-hours to stack 1 ton of a particular item. In order to stack 36 tons in 6 hours, the number of persons required is
questions.explanation: 36 tons needs 36×3 = 108 man-hours. In 6 hours, people needed = 108/6 = 18.

questions.question_text: Two office workers have been assigned to address 750 envelopes. One addresses twice as many envelopes per hour as the other. If it takes five hours for them to complete the job, what was the rate of the slower worker?
questions.explanation: Let slower worker = x envelopes/hr. Faster worker = 2x. Together for 5 hours: 5(3x)=750, so x=50.

questions.question_text: One printing press can do a job in 8 hours. Another printing press can do the same job in 12 hours. How long would it take both presses, working together, to do the job?
questions.explanation: Combined rate = 1/8 + 1/12 = 3/24 + 2/24 = 5/24 job/hr. Time = 24/5 = 4.8 hours = 4 hr 48 min.

questions.question_text: The length of a rectangle is 4 times the width. If the area of the rectangle is 324 square feet, the dimensions of the rectangle are
questions.explanation: Let width=x and length=4x. Area = 4x²=324, so x²=81, x=9. Length=36.

questions.explanation: Day 1: drive 300, remaining 2700. Day 2: drive 1/9 of 2700 =300, remaining 2400.

questions.question_text: Two children weighing 60 pounds and 80 pounds balance a seesaw. How many feet from the fulcrum must the heavier child sit if the lighter child is 8 feet from the fulcrum?
questions.explanation: Balance torques: 60×8 = 80x, so x=480/80=6 feet.

questions.question_text: If x varies directly as y² and if x=9 when y=2, what is x when y=8?
Two trains are 630 miles apart. At 9:00 am, they start traveling toward each other at average rates of 50 and 55 mph, respectively. At what time will they pass each other?
questions.explanation: Closing speed = 50+55=105 mph. Time=630/105=6 hours. 9:00 am + 6 hours = 3:00 pm.

questions.question_text: What is 10% of 40%?

conceptPages.title: Fractions & Exponents
conceptPages.overview: This lesson covers fraction arithmetic and exponent rules in a test-solving order. Fractions are about common denominators, KCF division, and mixed-number conversion. Exponents are about same-base rules, zero/negative exponents, and fractional exponents.

blocks.title: Start Here: Two Skills, One Topic
blocks.content:
This topic has two parts.

Part 1: Fractions.

You need to add, subtract, multiply, divide, and handle mixed numbers.

Part 2: Exponents.

You need same-base rules, negative exponents, and fractional exponents.

Teacher note:
These skills show up everywhere later: radicals, logs, quadratics, probability, and word problems.

blocks.title: Fractions: Adding and Subtracting Need Same Denominator
blocks.content:
You can only add or subtract fractions when the bottom numbers match.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\frac14+\frac16$$

The denominators are 4 and 6.

Find a shared denominator.

12 works.

$$\frac14=\frac3{12}$$

$$\frac16=\frac2{12}$$

Now add the top numbers only:

$$\frac3{12}+\frac2{12}=\frac5{12}$$

Teacher note:
Do not add denominators. The denominator stays the same after conversion.

blocks.title: Practice — Add Unlike Fractions
blocks.content:
Problem:
Simplify.

$$\frac56+\frac79$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Find the least common denominator.

6 and 9 both go into 18.

Step 2: Convert each fraction.

$$\frac56=\frac{15}{18}$$

$$\frac79=\frac{14}{18}$$

Step 3: Add the numerators.

$$\frac{15}{18}+\frac{14}{18}=\frac{29}{18}$$

Answer: $\frac{29}{18}$ or $1\frac{11}{18}$.

blocks.title: Multiplying Fractions: Straight Across
blocks.content:
For multiplication, you do not need a common denominator.

Multiply top × top and bottom × bottom.

$$\frac{a}{b}\times\frac{c}{d}=\frac{ac}{bd}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$\frac49\times\frac38$$

Cross-cancel first if possible.

4 and 8 reduce to 1 and 2.

3 and 9 reduce to 1 and 3.

So:

$$\frac13\times\frac12=\frac16$$

Teacher note:
Cross-canceling keeps the numbers small.

blocks.title: Dividing Fractions: KCF
blocks.content:
For fraction division, use KCF.

K = keep the first fraction.

C = change division to multiplication.

F = flip the second fraction.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\frac34\div\frac9{16}$$

Keep:

$$\frac34$$

Change and flip:

$$\frac34\times\frac{16}{9}$$

Multiply and simplify:

$$\frac{48}{36}=\frac43$$

Teacher note:
Only flip the second fraction, never the first.

blocks.title: Mixed Numbers: Convert First
blocks.content:
A mixed number has a whole number and a fraction.

Before multiplying or dividing, convert it to an improper fraction.

Formula:

$$a\frac{b}{c}=\frac{ac+b}{c}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$2\frac14$$

Multiply whole number by denominator:

$$2\times4=8$$

Add numerator:

$$8+1=9$$

Keep the denominator:

$$2\frac14=\frac94$$

Teacher note:
Mixed numbers are easier after conversion.

blocks.title: Practice — Mixed Number Multiplication
blocks.content:
Problem:
Simplify.

$$1\frac13\times2\frac14$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Convert both mixed numbers.

$$1\frac13=\frac43$$

$$2\frac14=\frac94$$

Step 2: Multiply.

$$\frac43\times\frac94$$

Cross-cancel 4 with 4.

$$\frac{1}{3}\times9=3$$

Answer: 3.

blocks.title: Exponent Rule 1: Same Base Multiplication
blocks.content:
When multiplying powers with the same base, add the exponents.

$$a^m\times a^n=a^{m+n}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$x^3\times x^5=x^{3+5}=x^8$$

Teacher note:
The base must match. This rule works for $x^3\times x^5$, not for $x^3\times y^5$.

blocks.title: Exponent Rule 2: Same Base Division
blocks.content:
When dividing powers with the same base, subtract the exponents.

$$\frac{a^m}{a^n}=a^{m-n}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$\frac{x^7}{x^2}=x^{7-2}=x^5$$

Teacher note:
Top exponent minus bottom exponent.

blocks.title: Exponent Rule 3: Power to a Power
blocks.content:
When raising a power to another power, multiply the exponents.

$$(a^m)^n=a^{mn}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$(x^2)^3=x^{2\times3}=x^6$$

Teacher note:
This is the rule where you multiply exponents.

blocks.title: Zero and Negative Exponents
blocks.content:
Zero exponent:

$$a^0=1$$

Negative exponent means move to the other side of the fraction.

$$a^{-n}=\frac1{a^n}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$2^{-3}=\frac1{2^3}=\frac18$$

Teacher note:
A negative exponent does not make the answer negative. It makes a reciprocal.

blocks.title: Practice — Negative Exponent
blocks.content:
Problem:
Simplify.

$$5^{-2}$$
blocks.solution:
Negative exponent means reciprocal.

$$5^{-2}=\frac1{5^2}$$

$$5^2=25$$

Answer: $\frac1{25}$.

blocks.title: Fractional Exponents: Denominator Root, Numerator Power
blocks.content:
Fractional exponents are roots and powers combined.

$$a^{m/n}=\left(\sqrt[n]{a}\right)^m$$

Denominator = root.

Numerator = power.

Tiny teaching example:

$$32^{3/5}$$

Denominator 5 means fifth root.

$$\sqrt[5]{32}=2$$

Numerator 3 means cube.

$$2^3=8$$

Teacher note:
Root first usually keeps the numbers smaller.

blocks.title: Practice — Fractional Exponent
blocks.content:
Problem:
Evaluate.

$$27^{2/3}$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Denominator 3 means cube root.

$$\sqrt[3]{27}=3$$

Step 2: Numerator 2 means square.

$$3^2=9$$

Answer: 9.

blocks.title: Negative Fractional Exponents
blocks.content:
A negative fractional exponent has two moves.

1. Negative means reciprocal.

2. Fraction means root and power.

Tiny teaching example:

$$16^{-3/4}$$

Negative means put it under 1:

$$\frac1{16^{3/4}}$$

Denominator 4 means fourth root:

$$\sqrt[4]{16}=2$$

Numerator 3 means cube:

$$2^3=8$$

So:

$$16^{-3/4}=\frac18$$

blocks.title: Practice — Negative Fractional Exponent
blocks.content:
Problem:
Evaluate.

$$32^{-2/5}$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Negative exponent means reciprocal.

$$32^{-2/5}=\frac1{32^{2/5}}$$

Step 2: Denominator 5 means fifth root.

$$\sqrt[5]{32}=2$$

Step 3: Numerator 2 means square.

$$2^2=4$$

Answer: $\frac14$.

blocks.title: Common Traps
blocks.content:
Trap 1: Adding denominators when adding fractions.

Trap 2: Forgetting KCF when dividing fractions.

Trap 3: Not converting mixed numbers before multiplying or dividing.

Trap 4: Thinking negative exponent means negative answer.

Trap 5: Multiplying exponents for same-base multiplication.

Correct: same-base multiplication means add exponents.

Trap 6: Doing power before root on fractional exponents and making the numbers huge.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Fractions & Exponents
blocks.content:
FRACTIONS

Add/subtract:

Same denominator first. Then add/subtract numerators.

Multiply:

$$\frac ab\times\frac cd=\frac{ac}{bd}$$

Divide:

KCF: keep, change, flip.

$$\frac ab\div\frac cd=\frac ab\times\frac dc$$

Mixed number:

$$a\frac bc=\frac{ac+b}{c}$$

EXPONENTS

Same base multiply:

$$a^m a^n=a^{m+n}$$

Same base divide:

$$\frac{a^m}{a^n}=a^{m-n}$$

Power to power:

$$(a^m)^n=a^{mn}$$

Zero exponent:

$$a^0=1$$

Negative exponent:

$$a^{-n}=\frac1{a^n}$$

Fractional exponent:

$$a^{m/n}=\left(\sqrt[n]{a}\right)^m$$

Negative fractional exponent:

$$a^{-m/n}=\frac1{(\sqrt[n]{a})^m}$$

key_formulas: FRACTIONS — division/KCF: $$\frac ab\div\frac cd=\frac ab\times\frac dc$$
common_mistakes: Treating $a^{-n}$ as a negative number — it means $\frac{1}{a^n}$, which is always positive when $a > 0$.
Computing power before root in $a^{m/n}$ — always take the root first to keep numbers small.
Adding denominators when adding fractions: $\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{4} \neq \frac{2}{7}$. Find the LCD first.
Forgetting to convert mixed numbers to improper fractions before multiplying or dividing.
Multiplying exponents when applying the product rule: $x^3 \times x^5 = x^8$, not $x^{15}$.
Forgetting that $a^0 = 1$ — when all exponents cancel, the result is 1, not 0.

conceptPages.title: Memorization Math
conceptPages.overview: This page is for facts that are faster to recognize than calculate. The goal is not to derive every fact during the test. The goal is to build quick recall for perfect numbers, powers, binary, fraction-decimal equivalents, and unit conversions.

blocks.title: Start Here: This Is a Lookup Table Topic
blocks.content:
Some OAR questions are not worth calculating from scratch.

If you see $13^2$, $7^3$, or a perfect number question, you want recognition, not a long calculation.

Teacher note:
This is different from algebra. Here, repetition is the study method.

blocks.title: Perfect Numbers
blocks.content:
A perfect number equals the sum of its proper divisors.

Proper divisors means factors smaller than the number itself.

Tiny teaching example:

For 6, the proper divisors are:

$$1,2,3$$

Add them:

$$1+2+3=6$$

So 6 is perfect.

The four to memorize are:

$$6,\ 28,\ 496,\ 8128$$

Teacher note:
On the test, do not try to prove every option. Recognize the list.

blocks.title: Practice — Perfect Number Recognition
blocks.content:
Problem:
Which of the following is a perfect number?

A) 130

B) 225

C) 330

D) 496
blocks.solution:
Use the memorized list:

$$6,\ 28,\ 496,\ 8128$$

The matching answer is 496.

Answer: D) 496.

Teacher note:
This is a recognition question. Do not waste time finding all divisors.

blocks.title: Perfect Squares: 1² through 15²
blocks.content:
Perfect squares show up in roots, geometry, quadratics, and Pythagorean problems.

Memorize these:

$$1^2=1,\quad2^2=4,\quad3^2=9,\quad4^2=16,\quad5^2=25$$

$$6^2=36,\quad7^2=49,\quad8^2=64,\quad9^2=81,\quad10^2=100$$

$$11^2=121,\quad12^2=144,\quad13^2=169,\quad14^2=196,\quad15^2=225$$

Number ending in 5 shortcut:

For $25^2$, multiply 2 by 3 and attach 25.

$$2\times3=6\Rightarrow625$$

blocks.title: Perfect Cubes: 1³ through 10³
blocks.content:
Perfect cubes show up in cube roots, radicals, and fractional exponents.

Memorize these:

$$1^3=1,\quad2^3=8,\quad3^3=27,\quad4^3=64,\quad5^3=125$$

$$6^3=216,\quad7^3=343,\quad8^3=512,\quad9^3=729,\quad10^3=1000$$

Teacher note:
The most annoying ones are 216, 343, 512, and 729. Give those extra repetition.

blocks.title: Fourth Powers: 1⁴ through 5⁴
blocks.content:
You only need the first five fourth powers.

$$1^4=1$$

$$2^4=16$$

$$3^4=81$$

$$4^4=256$$

$$5^4=625$$

Teacher note:
These are useful for fractional exponent questions.

blocks.title: Binary Representation
blocks.content:
Binary uses only 0 and 1.

Each place is a power of 2. From right to left:

$$1,2,4,8,16,32,\ldots$$

Tiny teaching example:
Convert 4 to binary.

4 is one 4, zero 2s, zero 1s.

So:

$$4=100_2$$

Common quick list:

$$0=0,\ 1=1,\ 2=10,\ 3=11,\ 4=100,\ 5=101$$

$$6=110,\ 7=111,\ 8=1000,\ 9=1001,\ 10=1010$$

Teacher note:
Binary is lower frequency, but if it appears, the first 10 values are enough for many questions.

blocks.title: Fraction-Decimal Equivalents
blocks.content:
These save time on percent, probability, and fraction questions.

Must know:

$$\frac12=0.5$$

$$\frac13\approx0.333,\quad\frac23\approx0.667$$

$$\frac14=0.25,\quad\frac34=0.75$$

$$\frac15=0.2,\quad\frac25=0.4,\quad\frac35=0.6,\quad\frac45=0.8$$

$$\frac18=0.125,\quad\frac38=0.375,\quad\frac58=0.625,\quad\frac78=0.875$$

Teacher note:
If you know quarters, fifths, and eighths, many percentage questions get easier.

blocks.title: Key Unit Conversions
blocks.content:
These are the common unit facts from the original lesson.

Length:

$$12\text{ inches}=1\text{ foot}$$

$$3\text{ feet}=1\text{ yard}$$

$$5280\text{ feet}=1\text{ mile}$$

Weight:

$$16\text{ ounces}=1\text{ pound}$$

$$2000\text{ pounds}=1\text{ ton}$$

Volume:

$$8\text{ pints}=1\text{ gallon}$$

$$4\text{ quarts}=1\text{ gallon}$$

Physics/math quick conversion:

$$60\text{ mph}=88\text{ ft/s}$$

$$1\text{ kg}\approx9.81\text{ N of weight on Earth}$$

Teacher note:
Do not confuse 5280 feet per mile with 3600 seconds per hour.

blocks.title: Common Traps
blocks.content:
Trap 1: Thinking 12, 36, or 64 are perfect numbers. They are not.

Trap 2: Forgetting $9^3=729$.

Trap 3: Mixing up $2^3=8$ and $2^4=16$.

Trap 4: Using 3600 feet for one mile. One mile is 5280 feet.

Trap 5: Trying to calculate perfect numbers during the test instead of memorizing the list.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper / Flashcard Checklist — Memorization Math

key_formulas: UNITS: $$5280\text{ ft}=1\text{ mi},\ 16\text{ oz}=1\text{ lb},\ 2000\text{ lb}=1\text{ ton}$$
common_mistakes: Confusing $9^3 = 729$ with 810 — remember $81 \times 9 = 720 + 9 = 729$.
Confusing $2^3 = 8$ with $2^4 = 16$ — the fourth power of 2 is 16, not 8.
Believing 12, 36, or 64 are perfect numbers — only 6, 28, 496, and 8128 are.
Forgetting $\frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167$ and $\frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$ — memorize these as a pair.
Using $3{,}600$ ft instead of $5{,}280$ ft for one mile — $5{,}280$ is the correct value.

conceptPages.title: Percentages & Percent Change
conceptPages.overview: Percent questions are part-whole problems. This lesson teaches the three core percent types, percent change, discount/markup, successive discounts, depreciation, spending chains, percent of a percent, and simple interest in a test-solving order.

blocks.title: Start Here: Percent Means Out of 100
blocks.content:
Percent means per hundred.

$$45\%=\frac{45}{100}=0.45$$

Most percent problems are asking for one missing piece:

Amount, percent, or whole.

Teacher note:
Before solving, ask: What is the part? What is the whole? What percent is being used?

blocks.title: The Main Percent Relationship
blocks.content:
All three core percent types come from one relationship.

$$\text{Amount}=\text{Percent as decimal}\times\text{Whole}$$

Tiny teaching example:

What is 30% of 250?

30% = 0.30.

$$0.30\times250=75$$

Teacher note:
“Of” usually means multiply.

blocks.title: Type 1: Find the Amount
blocks.content:
Use this when the question asks:

What is ___% of ___?

Formula:

$$\text{Amount}=\text{Percent}\times\text{Whole}$$

Tiny teaching example:

What is 20% of 80?

$$0.20\times80=16$$

Answer: 16.

blocks.title: Type 2: Find the Percent
blocks.content:
Use this when the question asks:

___ is what percent of ___?

Formula:

$$\text{Percent}=\frac{\text{Amount}}{\text{Whole}}\times100$$

Tiny teaching example:

45 is what percent of 180?

$$\frac{45}{180}\times100=25\%$$

Teacher note:
The number after “of” is usually the whole.

blocks.title: Type 3: Find the Whole
blocks.content:
Use this when the question asks:

___ is ___% of what number?

Formula:

$$\text{Whole}=\frac{\text{Amount}}{\text{Percent as decimal}}$$

Tiny teaching example:

60 is 40% of what number?

$$\frac{60}{0.40}=150$$

Answer: 150.

blocks.title: Practice — Three Core Percent Types
blocks.content:
Problem:
75 is what percent of 300?
blocks.solution:
This asks for the percent.

Use:

$$\text{Percent}=\frac{\text{Amount}}{\text{Whole}}\times100$$

Substitute:

$$\frac{75}{300}\times100$$

$$0.25\times100=25\%$$

Answer: 25%.

blocks.title: Percent Increase and Decrease
blocks.content:
Percent change always uses the original value as the base.

Formula:

$$\text{Percent change}=\frac{\text{New}-\text{Original}}{\text{Original}}\times100$$

Tiny teaching example:

A score goes from 80 to 100.

Change:

$$100-80=20$$

Divide by original:

$$\frac{20}{80}=0.25$$

Convert to percent:

$$25\%$$

Teacher note:
Do not divide by the new value.

blocks.title: Discount and Sale Price
blocks.content:
For discount problems, multiply by what remains.

If something is 20% off, you pay 80%.

Formula:

$$\text{Sale price}=\text{Original}\times(1-r)$$

Tiny teaching example:

20% off $65.

Remain multiplier:

$$1-0.20=0.80$$

Sale price:

$$65\times0.80=52$$

Answer: $52.

blocks.title: Finding Original Price From Sale Price
blocks.content:
If you know the sale price and the discount, divide by what remains.

Formula:

$$\text{Original}=\frac{\text{Sale price}}{1-r}$$

Tiny teaching example:

A sale price is $48 after 25% off. What was the original price?

You paid 75% of the original.

$$1-0.25=0.75$$

$$\frac{48}{0.75}=64$$

Answer: $64.

blocks.title: Markup and Selling Price
blocks.content:
Markup means the price increases above cost.

Formula:

$$\text{Selling price}=\text{Cost}\times(1+\text{markup rate})$$

Tiny teaching example:

Cost is $40. Markup is 35%.

Multiplier:

$$1+0.35=1.35$$

Selling price:

$$40\times1.35=54$$

Answer: $54.

blocks.title: Successive Discounts Are Not Added
blocks.content:
If there are two discounts, apply them one at a time.

A 20% discount followed by a 10% discount is not 30% off.

Tiny teaching example:

Start with $100.

20% off means keep 80%.

$$100\times0.80=80$$

Then 10% off means keep 90% of 80.

$$80\times0.90=72$$

You paid $72, so the total discount is 28%.

Teacher note:
Successive discounts multiply the remaining fractions.

blocks.title: Practice — Successive Discount
blocks.content:
Problem:
A $100 item has successive discounts of 20% and 15%. What is the final price?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: First discount.

20% off means keep 80%.

$$100\times0.80=80$$

Step 2: Second discount.

15% off means keep 85%.

$$80\times0.85=68$$

Answer: $68.

Teacher note:
The total discount is $32, so it is 32% off. Not 35%.

blocks.title: Compound Depreciation / Repeated Percent Change
blocks.content:
When the same percent decrease happens repeatedly, use the remaining multiplier repeatedly.

Formula:

$$\text{Value}=P(1-r)^n$$

Tiny teaching example:

A $20,000 vehicle depreciates 10% per year for 3 years.

Keep 90% each year.

$$20000(0.90)^3$$

$$0.90^3=0.729$$

$$20000(0.729)=14580$$

Answer: $14,580.

blocks.title: Fractional Spending Chains
blocks.content:
In spending-chain problems, watch for the word remainder.

Tiny teaching example:

A woman spends $\frac15$ of her income on rent, then $\frac38$ of the remainder on food. What fraction is left?

After rent, she has:

$$1-\frac15=\frac45$$

Food is $\frac38$ of the remainder:

$$\frac38\times\frac45=\frac{12}{40}=\frac3{10}$$

Total spent:

$$\frac15+\frac3{10}=\frac2{10}+\frac3{10}=\frac5{10}=\frac12$$

Remaining:

$$1-\frac12=\frac12$$

Teacher note:
A fraction of the remainder is not a fraction of the original total.

blocks.title: Percent of a Percent
blocks.content:
Percent of a percent means multiply the decimals.

Tiny teaching example:

30% of 40% of 500.

Work from right to left:

$$40\%\text{ of }500=0.40(500)=200$$

Then:

$$30\%\text{ of }200=0.30(200)=60$$

Shortcut:

$$0.30\times0.40\times500=60$$

blocks.title: Simple Interest
blocks.content:
Simple interest uses the original principal only.

Formula:

$$I=Prt$$

$I$ = interest.

$P$ = principal, or starting amount.

$r$ = annual rate as a decimal.

$t$ = time in years.

Tiny teaching example:

$2,000 at 5% simple interest for 3 years.

$$I=2000(0.05)(3)=300$$

Total amount:

$$2000+300=2300$$

Teacher note:
Simple interest does not grow the principal each year.

blocks.title: Common Traps
blocks.content:
Trap 1: Dividing by the new value for percent change.

Use the original.

Trap 2: Adding successive discount rates.

Multiply what remains.

Trap 3: Applying a fraction to the total when the problem says remainder.

Trap 4: Confusing simple interest with compound interest.

Simple interest uses the original principal only.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Percentages
blocks.content:
CORE PERCENT

Amount:

$$\text{Amount}=\text{Percent}\times\text{Whole}$$

Percent:

$$\text{Percent}=\frac{\text{Amount}}{\text{Whole}}\times100$$

Whole:

$$\text{Whole}=\frac{\text{Amount}}{\text{Percent as decimal}}$$

PERCENT CHANGE

$$\frac{\text{New}-\text{Original}}{\text{Original}}\times100$$

DISCOUNT

Sale price:

$$\text{Original}(1-r)$$

Original from sale:

$$\frac{\text{Sale price}}{1-r}$$

MARKUP

$$\text{Cost}(1+r)$$

SUCCESSIVE DISCOUNTS

Remaining:

$$(1-d_1)(1-d_2)$$

COMPOUND DEPRECIATION

$$P(1-r)^n$$

SIMPLE INTEREST

$$I=Prt$$

common_mistakes: Using the new value as the base for percent change — always divide by the ORIGINAL.
Adding successive discount rates: a 20% then 10% discount is 28% off, not 30%.
Applying a fraction to the total instead of the remainder in a spending chain.
Confusing simple interest (fixed principal) with compound interest (growing principal).

conceptPages.title: Exponent Rules
conceptPages.overview: Exponent rules are shortcuts for repeated multiplication. For OAR, the goal is to recognize which rule applies: same base multiply, same base divide, power to a power, zero exponent, negative exponent, or distributing an exponent to every factor.

blocks.title: Start Here: What an Exponent Means
blocks.content:
An exponent tells you how many times to multiply a base by itself.

Example:

$$x^3=x\cdot x\cdot x$$

Exponent rules let you avoid writing everything out.

Tiny teaching example:

$$x^3\cdot x^5$$

This means 3 x's times 5 more x's.

Total x's:

$$3+5=8$$

So:

$$x^3\cdot x^5=x^8$$

Teacher note:
Exponent rules are only safe when the bases match.

blocks.title: Rule 1: Product Rule
blocks.content:
Use the product rule when multiplying the same base.

Same base means the bottom letter or number is the same.

Rule:

$$x^m\cdot x^n=x^{m+n}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$x^3\cdot x^5=x^{3+5}=x^8$$

Important:

$$x^3\cdot y^5$$

cannot combine because the bases are different.

Teacher note:
Same base + multiply = add exponents.

blocks.title: Practice — Product Rule
blocks.content:
Problem:
Simplify.

$$a^4\cdot a^7$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Check the base.

Both bases are a.

Step 2: Multiplying same base means add exponents.

$$a^4\cdot a^7=a^{4+7}$$

$$a^{11}$$

Answer:

$$a^{11}$$

blocks.title: Rule 2: Quotient Rule
blocks.content:
Use the quotient rule when dividing the same base.

Rule:

$$\frac{x^m}{x^n}=x^{m-n}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$\frac{x^7}{x^3}=x^{7-3}=x^4$$

Teacher note:
Same base + divide = subtract exponents. The order is top minus bottom.

blocks.title: Practice — Quotient Rule
blocks.content:
Problem:
Simplify.

$$\frac{m^9}{m^2}$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Check the base.

Both bases are m.

Step 2: Dividing same base means subtract exponents.

$$\frac{m^9}{m^2}=m^{9-2}$$

$$m^7$$

Answer:

$$m^7$$

Teacher note:
Do top exponent minus bottom exponent.

blocks.title: Rule 3: Power Rule
blocks.content:
Use the power rule when a power is raised to another power.

Rule:

$$(x^m)^n=x^{mn}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$(x^2)^5=x^{2\cdot5}=x^{10}$$

Teacher note:
Power to a power means multiply exponents.

blocks.title: Practice — Power Rule
blocks.content:
Problem:
Simplify.

$$(y^3)^4$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: This is a power raised to another power.

Step 2: Multiply exponents.

$$(y^3)^4=y^{3\cdot4}$$

$$y^{12}$$

Answer:

$$y^{12}$$

blocks.title: Zero Exponent
blocks.content:
Any nonzero base to the zero power equals 1.

Rule:

$$a^0=1$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$5^0=1$$

$$x^0=1$$

Why?

$$\frac{x^5}{x^5}=1$$

Using exponent rules:

$$\frac{x^5}{x^5}=x^{5-5}=x^0$$

So:

$$x^0=1$$

Teacher note:
Zero exponent does not mean 0. It means 1.

blocks.title: Negative Exponents
blocks.content:
A negative exponent means move to the other side of the fraction.

Rule:

$$a^{-n}=\frac{1}{a^n}$$

Tiny teaching examples:

$$x^{-3}=\frac{1}{x^3}$$

$$2^{-4}=\frac{1}{2^4}=\frac{1}{16}$$

Teacher note:
Negative exponent does not make the number negative. It creates a reciprocal.

blocks.title: Subtracting a Negative Exponent
blocks.content:
This is a common trap.

When the bottom exponent is negative, quotient rule still means top minus bottom.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\frac{x^3}{x^{-2}}$$

Use quotient rule:

$$x^{3-(-2)}$$

Subtracting a negative becomes addition:

$$x^{3+2}=x^5$$

Teacher note:
Top minus bottom still works. Just be careful with the negative sign.

blocks.title: Power of a Product
blocks.content:
When an exponent is outside parentheses, it applies to every factor inside.

Rule:

$$(ab)^n=a^n b^n$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$(2x^3)^4$$

The 4 applies to the 2 and to $x^3$.

$$2^4\cdot(x^3)^4$$

$$16x^{12}$$

Teacher note:
Do not forget the coefficient. $(2x^3)^4$ is not $2x^{12}$.

blocks.title: Practice — Power of a Product
blocks.content:
Problem:
Simplify.

$$(3x^2y^3)^2$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Apply the outside exponent to every factor.

$$3^2\cdot(x^2)^2\cdot(y^3)^2$$

Step 2: Simplify each part.

$$3^2=9$$

$$(x^2)^2=x^4$$

$$(y^3)^2=y^6$$

Answer:

$$9x^4y^6$$

Teacher note:
Handle the coefficient, x, and y separately.

blocks.title: Practice — Multiple Rules Together
blocks.content:
Problem:
Simplify.

$$\frac{x^3\cdot x^{-5}}{x^{-2}}$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Simplify the top first.

$$x^3\cdot x^{-5}=x^{3+(-5)}=x^{-2}$$

Step 2: Divide by the bottom.

$$\frac{x^{-2}}{x^{-2}}=x^{-2-(-2)}$$

Step 3: Subtracting a negative becomes addition.

$$x^{-2+2}=x^0$$

Step 4: Use zero exponent.

$$x^0=1$$

Answer:

$$1$$

blocks.title: Multi-Variable Expressions
blocks.content:
For expressions with coefficients and multiple variables, handle each part separately.

Order:

1. Coefficients.

2. x powers.

3. y powers.

4. Any other variables.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\frac{9x^4y^{-6}}{9x^{-1}y}$$

Coefficients:

$$\frac{9}{9}=1$$

x powers:

$$x^{4-(-1)}=x^5$$

y powers:

$$y^{-6-1}=y^{-7}=\frac{1}{y^7}$$

Answer:

$$\frac{x^5}{y^7}$$

Teacher note:
Do not try to do everything in one mental step.

blocks.title: Sums of Powers Divided by a Power
blocks.content:
When you see powers being added, you cannot just subtract exponents across the whole sum.

Use factoring first.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\frac{2^8+2^9+2^{10}}{2^7}$$

Factor the smallest power from the top:

$$2^8(1+2+4)$$

$$2^8(7)$$

Now divide:

$$\frac{2^8\cdot7}{2^7}=2^{8-7}\cdot7$$

$$2\cdot7=14$$

Teacher note:
For sums, factor first. Do not use quotient rule across addition.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Exponent Rules
blocks.content:
Keep this at the end as a summary.

PRODUCT RULE — multiply same base:

$$x^m\cdot x^n=x^{m+n}$$

QUOTIENT RULE — divide same base:

$$\frac{x^m}{x^n}=x^{m-n}$$

POWER RULE — power to a power:

$$(x^m)^n=x^{mn}$$

ZERO EXPONENT:

$$a^0=1$$

NEGATIVE EXPONENT:

$$a^{-n}=\frac{1}{a^n}$$

POWER OF A PRODUCT:

$$(ab)^n=a^n b^n$$

METHOD FLOW

Multiplying same base: add exponents.

Dividing same base: subtract top minus bottom.

Power to a power: multiply exponents.

Negative exponent: move to other side of fraction.

Outside exponent on parentheses: apply to everything inside.

Sums of powers: factor first.

Teacher note:
Always identify the structure first. Do not blindly add exponents.

common_mistakes: Adding exponents when the bases are different.
Subtracting bottom minus top instead of top minus bottom.
Thinking a zero exponent equals zero.
Thinking a negative exponent makes the answer negative.
Forgetting to raise the coefficient in power-of-a-product problems.
Using quotient rule across a sum instead of factoring first.

conceptPages.title: Radicals & Roots
conceptPages.overview: Radicals are roots. This lesson teaches what the radical is asking, how to simplify roots, combine like radicals, multiply radicals, rationalize denominators, handle variables under roots, and solve radical equations.

blocks.title: Start Here: What Is a Radical?
blocks.content:
A radical asks: what number was multiplied by itself?

Square root:

$$\sqrt9=3$$

because:

$$3^2=9$$

Cube root:

$$\sqrt[3]{8}=2$$

because:

$$2^3=8$$

The number inside the radical is called the radicand.

If there is no small index number, it is a square root.

Teacher note:
A radical is connected to fractional exponents: $\sqrt{x}=x^{1/2}$ and $\sqrt[3]{x}=x^{1/3}$.

blocks.title: Simplifying Square Roots: Pull Out Perfect Squares
blocks.content:
To simplify a square root, find a perfect square factor inside it.

Perfect squares include:

$$4,9,16,25,36,49,64,81,100$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$\sqrt{50}$$

Find a perfect square inside 50.

$$50=25\times2$$

Split it:

$$\sqrt{50}=\sqrt{25\times2}$$

Pull out $\sqrt{25}=5$:

$$\sqrt{50}=5\sqrt2$$

Teacher note:
Use the largest perfect square you can find. It saves steps.

blocks.title: Practice — Simplify a Square Root
blocks.content:
Problem:
Simplify.

$$\sqrt{72}$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Find a perfect square factor.

$$72=36\times2$$

Step 2: Split the radical.

$$\sqrt{72}=\sqrt{36\times2}$$

Step 3: Pull out the square root of 36.

$$\sqrt{36}=6$$

So:

$$\sqrt{72}=6\sqrt2$$

Answer: $6\sqrt2$.

blocks.title: Cube Roots and Decimal Cube Roots
blocks.content:
Cube root asks what number cubed gives the radicand.

Common cube roots:

$$\sqrt[3]{1}=1,\quad\sqrt[3]{8}=2,\quad\sqrt[3]{27}=3,\quad\sqrt[3]{64}=4,\quad\sqrt[3]{125}=5$$

$$\sqrt[3]{216}=6,\quad\sqrt[3]{343}=7,\quad\sqrt[3]{512}=8,\quad\sqrt[3]{729}=9,\quad\sqrt[3]{1000}=10$$

Decimal cube root example:

$$\sqrt[3]{0.027}=0.3$$

because:

$$0.3^3=0.027$$

Another way:

$$0.027=\frac{27}{1000}$$

$$\sqrt[3]{\frac{27}{1000}}=\frac3{10}=0.3$$

blocks.title: Adding and Subtracting Radicals: Like Terms Only
blocks.content:
You can add or subtract radicals only when the radicand matches after simplifying.

Radicand means the number under the radical.

Tiny teaching example:

$$2\sqrt3+5\sqrt3$$

The radical part matches, so add the coefficients.

$$2\sqrt3+5\sqrt3=7\sqrt3$$

But:

$$\sqrt2+\sqrt3$$

cannot be combined.

Teacher note:
Treat $\sqrt3$ like a variable term. $2\sqrt3+5\sqrt3=7\sqrt3$.

blocks.title: Practice — Add Radicals After Simplifying
blocks.content:
Problem:
Simplify.

$$\sqrt{18}+\sqrt8$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Simplify each radical.

$$\sqrt{18}=\sqrt{9\times2}=3\sqrt2$$

$$\sqrt8=\sqrt{4\times2}=2\sqrt2$$

Step 2: Now the radical parts match.

$$3\sqrt2+2\sqrt2=5\sqrt2$$

Answer: $5\sqrt2$.

Teacher note:
At first, 18 and 8 do not match. After simplifying, both become $\sqrt2$ terms.

blocks.title: Multiplying Radicals
blocks.content:
When multiplying radicals with the same index, multiply the numbers inside.

$$\sqrt a\cdot\sqrt b=\sqrt{ab}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$\sqrt3\cdot\sqrt6=\sqrt{18}$$

Then simplify:

$$\sqrt{18}=3\sqrt2$$

With coefficients:

$$(2\sqrt3)(4\sqrt5)=8\sqrt{15}$$

Teacher note:
Multiply outside numbers with outside numbers, and inside numbers with inside numbers.

blocks.title: Rationalizing a Single Radical Denominator
blocks.content:
Rationalizing means removing the radical from the denominator.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\frac6{\sqrt3}$$

Multiply top and bottom by $\sqrt3$:

$$\frac6{\sqrt3}\cdot\frac{\sqrt3}{\sqrt3}=\frac{6\sqrt3}{3}$$

Simplify:

$$2\sqrt3$$

Teacher note:
You are multiplying by 1, so you are changing the form, not the value.

blocks.title: Rationalizing with a Conjugate
blocks.content:
If the denominator has two terms with a radical, use the conjugate.

Conjugate means same two terms, opposite sign.

For:

$$\sqrt a+\sqrt b$$

The conjugate is:

$$\sqrt a-\sqrt b$$

Key pattern:

$$(\sqrt a+\sqrt b)(\sqrt a-\sqrt b)=a-b$$

Teacher note:
This removes radicals from the denominator because middle terms cancel.

blocks.title: Variables Under a Radical
blocks.content:
For variables, pull out pairs for square roots.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\sqrt{x^6}$$

Square root asks for pairs.

$$x^6=(x^3)^2$$

So:

$$\sqrt{x^6}=x^3$$

Another example:

$$\sqrt{9x^4}=3x^2$$

Teacher note:
For OAR-style simplification, pull out even powers by cutting the exponent in half.

blocks.title: Solving Radical Equations
blocks.content:
For equations with radicals, use this flow.

1. Isolate the radical.

2. Square both sides.

3. Solve.

4. Check the answer.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\sqrt x=4$$

Square both sides:

$$x=16$$

Check:

$$\sqrt{16}=4$$

Teacher note:
Checking matters because squaring both sides can create false answers.

blocks.title: Practice — Radical Equation
blocks.content:
Problem:
Solve.

$$(4\sqrt x-3)^2=0$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: If a square equals 0, the inside must equal 0.

$$4\sqrt x-3=0$$

Step 2: Isolate the radical.

$$4\sqrt x=3$$

$$\sqrt x=\frac34$$

Step 3: Square both sides.

$$x=\left(\frac34\right)^2=\frac9{16}$$

Step 4: Check.

$$4\sqrt{\frac9{16}}-3=4\left(\frac34\right)-3=0$$

Answer: $\frac9{16}$.

blocks.title: Common Traps
blocks.content:
Trap 1: Thinking $\sqrt{a+b}=\sqrt a+\sqrt b$.

That is false.

Trap 2: Combining radicals before simplifying.

Trap 3: Adding radicals with different radicands.

Trap 4: Leaving a radical in the denominator when the answer choices are simplified.

Trap 5: Not checking radical equation answers after squaring.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Radicals & Roots
blocks.content:
MEANING

$$\sqrt{x}=x^{1/2}$$

$$\sqrt[3]{x}=x^{1/3}$$

SIMPLIFY SQUARE ROOT

Find perfect square factor.

$$\sqrt{ab}=\sqrt a\sqrt b$$

ADD/SUBTRACT RADICALS

Only combine if radicands match.

$$a\sqrt x+b\sqrt x=(a+b)\sqrt x$$

MULTIPLY RADICALS

$$(a\sqrt m)(b\sqrt n)=ab\sqrt{mn}$$

RATIONALIZE SINGLE RADICAL

$$\frac{k}{\sqrt n}=\frac{k\sqrt n}{n}$$

CONJUGATE

$$(\sqrt a+\sqrt b)(\sqrt a-\sqrt b)=a-b$$

VARIABLES

$$\sqrt{x^{2k}}=x^k$$

SOLVE RADICAL EQUATION

Isolate radical → square both sides → solve → check.

key_formulas: ADD LIKE RADICALS ONLY: $$a\sqrt x+b\sqrt x=(a+b)\sqrt x$$
common_mistakes: $\sqrt{a+b} \neq \sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b}$ — the product rule works under a radical, addition does not.
Combining radicals without simplifying first — always simplify to check if radicands match.
Leaving a radical in the denominator — rationalize by multiplying by the radical (or its conjugate).
Not checking solutions after squaring both sides — squaring can create extraneous solutions.

conceptPages.title: Averages
conceptPages.overview: Average problems are usually total problems in disguise. The fastest OAR strategy is to turn averages into sums first, then solve from the total.

blocks.title: Start Here: Average Means Total Divided by Count
blocks.content:
Average means:

$$\text{Average}=\frac{\text{Sum}}{\text{Count}}$$

But for OAR, the more useful version is:

$$\text{Sum}=\text{Average}\times\text{Count}$$

Tiny teaching example:

If 5 tests have an average of 80, then the total points must be:

$$80\times5=400$$

Teacher note:
For average problems, convert to total first. Totals are easier to work with.

blocks.title: Method 1: Missing Score
blocks.content:
Use this when the question gives several scores and asks what score is needed.

Steps:

1. Find the required total.

2. Add the known scores.

3. Subtract known total from required total.

Tiny teaching example:

Scores are 72, 85, 90, and 78. What fifth score gives an average of 82?

Required total:

$$82\times5=410$$

Known total:

$$72+85+90+78=325$$

Missing score:

$$410-325=85$$

Teacher note:
Do not average the known scores first. Find the total needed.

blocks.title: Practice — Missing Score
blocks.content:
Problem:
A student scored 70, 80, 90, and 85 on four tests. What score is needed on the fifth test to average 82?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Find the required total for 5 tests.

$$82\times5=410$$

Step 2: Add the known scores.

$$70+80+90+85=325$$

Step 3: Subtract.

$$410-325=85$$

Answer: 85.

Teacher note:
Average questions become easier when you turn them into total questions.

blocks.title: Method 2: Weighted Averages
blocks.content:
Weighted average means not everything counts equally.

Use:

$$\frac{\text{total weighted points}}{\text{total weight}}$$

If the weights are percentages that add to 100%, multiply each score by its percent and add.

Tiny teaching example:

Exam 1 is 30% and score is 80.

Exam 2 is 40% and score is 75.

Final is 30% and score is 90.

Weighted grade:

$$0.30(80)+0.40(75)+0.30(90)$$

$$24+30+27=81$$

Teacher note:
A weighted average is not always just adding scores and dividing by the number of scores.

blocks.title: Practice — Weighted Grade
blocks.content:
Problem:
A course grade is 20% homework, 30% midterm, and 50% final. The scores are 90, 80, and 70. What is the final course grade?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Convert percentages to decimals.

20% = 0.20

30% = 0.30

50% = 0.50

Step 2: Multiply each score by its weight.

$$0.20(90)=18$$

$$0.30(80)=24$$

$$0.50(70)=35$$

Step 3: Add.

$$18+24+35=77$$

Answer: 77.

Teacher note:
The final counts most because it has the biggest weight.

blocks.title: Method 3: Combined Group Average
blocks.content:
Use this when groups have different sizes.

Do not average the averages directly unless the groups are the same size.

Tiny teaching example:

Team A has 6 workers averaging 40 boxes per hour.

Team B has 4 workers averaging 55 boxes per hour.

Find total boxes first.

Team A total:

$$6\times40=240$$

Team B total:

$$4\times55=220$$

Total boxes:

$$240+220=460$$

Total workers:

$$6+4=10$$

Combined average:

$$460\div10=46$$

Teacher note:
Group size is the weight.

blocks.title: Practice — Combined Group Average
blocks.content:
Problem:
Group A has 10 people with an average score of 70. Group B has 5 people with an average score of 90. What is the combined average?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Convert each average into a total.

Group A total:

$$10\times70=700$$

Group B total:

$$5\times90=450$$

Step 2: Add totals.

$$700+450=1150$$

Step 3: Add people.

$$10+5=15$$

Step 4: Divide total by count.

$$1150\div15=76.67$$

Answer: about 76.7.

Teacher note:
The answer is closer to 70 than 90 because Group A is larger.

blocks.title: Method 4: Fractionally Defined Score
blocks.content:
Sometimes one score is defined as a fraction of another score or average.

Do the referenced average first.

Tiny teaching example:

Exam 3 equals $\frac{2}{3}$ of the average of Exam 1 and Exam 2.

Exam 1 = 75.

Exam 2 = 60.

First find the average of Exam 1 and Exam 2:

$$\frac{75+60}{2}=67.5$$

Now take $\frac{2}{3}$ of that:

$$\frac{2}{3}(67.5)=45$$

Teacher note:
When a score depends on another average, calculate the base first.

blocks.title: Method 5: Nested Averages
blocks.content:
Nested averages are average problems inside average problems.

The same rule still works:

Convert averages into sums.

Tiny teaching example:

The class average was 28.6. That is 0.6 lower than the average of Susan and Julie. Susan scored 18 lower than Julie.

Average of Susan and Julie:

$$28.6+0.6=29.2$$

Sum of Susan and Julie:

$$29.2\times2=58.4$$

Let Julie = J.

Susan = J - 18.

Equation:

$$(J-18)+J=58.4$$

$$2J-18=58.4$$

$$2J=76.4$$

$$J=38.2$$

Teacher note:
For nested averages, every average becomes a total.

blocks.title: Method 6: Required Future Scores
blocks.content:
Use this when the question gives a target average and asks what future score is needed.

Tiny teaching example:

A student needs an average of 80 across 7 tests. The first 5 scores add to 406.

Required total:

$$80\times7=560$$

Scores 6 and 7 must add to:

$$560-406=154$$

If test 6 equals $\frac{4}{5}$ of test 7, let test 7 = t.

Then test 6:

$$\frac{4}{5}t$$

Equation:

$$\frac{4}{5}t+t=154$$

Teacher note:
First find the missing total. Then handle the relationship between the unknown scores.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Averages
blocks.content:
Keep this at the end as a summary.

BASIC AVERAGE

$$\text{Average}=\frac{\text{Sum}}{\text{Count}}$$

OAR VERSION

$$\text{Sum}=\text{Average}\times\text{Count}$$

MISSING SCORE

$$\text{Missing}=\text{Required Total}-\text{Known Total}$$

WEIGHTED AVERAGE

$$\text{Weighted Average}=\frac{\sum(\text{value}\times\text{weight})}{\sum\text{weights}}$$

COMBINED GROUP AVERAGE

$$\frac{n_1\bar{x}_1+n_2\bar{x}_2}{n_1+n_2}$$

METHOD FLOW

If target average is given, find required total.

If group sizes are given, convert each group to a total.

If weights are percentages, multiply each score by its percent.

If one score depends on another, calculate the base first.

Teacher note:
Average = total divided by count. Most OAR average problems are easier if you work backward from the total.

common_mistakes: Averaging averages directly when group sizes are different.
Forgetting to multiply average by count to get total.
Applying a fraction to the wrong base.
Trying to set up a complicated equation before finding the required total.

conceptPages.title: Word-to-Equation Translation
conceptPages.overview: Word-to-equation translation means turning English into algebra. For OAR, this is one of the most important skills because many word problems are not hard because of calculation — they are hard because the sentence is easy to misread.

blocks.title: Start Here: Translate Before Solving
blocks.content:
Every word problem has two jobs.

Job 1: Translate the sentence into math.

Job 2: Solve the math.

Do not rush into solving before you know what the sentence means.

Tiny teaching example:

“Three more than a number is 10.”

A number = x.

Three more than the number = x + 3.

Is = equals.

Equation:

$$x+3=10$$

Then solve:

$$x=7$$

Teacher note:
Most word-problem mistakes happen during translation, not during algebra.

blocks.title: Phrase Dictionary
blocks.content:
Use this dictionary to translate phrases.

“is,” “equals,” “is the same as” means:

$$=$$

“sum,” “plus,” “increased by” means:

$$+$$

“minus,” “difference,” “decreased by” means:

$$-$$

“times,” “product,” and “of” with a fraction or percent means:

$$\times$$

“divided by,” “quotient,” “per,” and “ratio of” means:

$$\div$$

“a number,” “some number,” or “what number” usually means:

$$x$$

Teacher note:
Do not memorize this as a fancy table. Memorize it as a translation dictionary.

blocks.title: The Dangerous Phrases: Less Than, More Than, Exceeds By
blocks.content:
Some phrases reverse the order.

“A less than B” means:

$$B-A$$

Example:

“Three less than twice a number.”

Twice a number:

$$2x$$

Three less than that:

$$2x-3$$

Not:

$$3-2x$$

“A exceeds B by C” means:

$$A-B=C$$

Plain English:
A is bigger than B by C.

Teacher note:
For “less than,” look at what comes after “than.” That is the starting amount.

blocks.title: Practice — Exceeds By
blocks.content:
Problem:
Translate the sentence.

“Two times r exceeds one-half of t by 5.”
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Translate the first quantity.

Two times r:

$$2r$$

Step 2: Translate the second quantity.

One-half of t:

$$\frac{1}{2}t$$

Step 3: Use the exceeds-by pattern.

“A exceeds B by C” means:

$$A-B=C$$

Step 4: Substitute.

$$2r-\frac{1}{2}t=5$$

Answer:

$$2r-\frac{1}{2}t=5$$

Teacher note:
The first quantity comes first. Do not reverse it.

blocks.title: Number Relationship Problems
blocks.content:
A number relationship problem describes an unknown number.

Start by letting the unknown be x.

Tiny teaching example:

“Five more than three times a number equals twice the number plus 20.”

Three times a number:

$$3x$$

Five more than that:

$$3x+5$$

Twice the number plus 20:

$$2x+20$$

Equation:

$$3x+5=2x+20$$

Solve:

$$x=15$$

Teacher note:
Translate one chunk at a time. Do not try to hold the whole sentence in your head.

blocks.title: Consecutive Integer Problems
blocks.content:
Consecutive means numbers that come one after another.

Consecutive integers:

$$n,\ n+1,\ n+2$$

Consecutive even integers:

$$n,\ n+2,\ n+4$$

Consecutive odd integers:

$$n,\ n+2,\ n+4$$

Tiny teaching example:

“The sum of three consecutive integers is 48.”

Set them up:

$$n+(n+1)+(n+2)=48$$

Combine:

$$3n+3=48$$

$$3n=45$$

$$n=15$$

Numbers:

$$15,16,17$$

Teacher note:
Even and odd consecutive numbers both jump by 2. The starting number controls whether they are even or odd.

blocks.title: Age Problems
blocks.content:
Age problems are translation problems with time.

Let the letters mean current ages first.

Future: add the same number of years to everyone.

Past: subtract the same number of years from everyone.

Tiny teaching example:

Abigail is 6 years older than Jonathan. Six years ago, Abigail was twice as old as Jonathan.

Let:

J = Jonathan now.

A = Abigail now.

Current condition:

$$A=J+6$$

Six years ago:

Abigail was:

$$A-6$$

Jonathan was:

$$J-6$$

Twice as old means:

$$A-6=2(J-6)$$

Teacher note:
Time changes everyone. If the problem says six years ago, subtract 6 from both ages.

blocks.title: Practice — Age Problem
blocks.content:
Problem:
A mother is currently 3 times as old as her daughter. In 5 years, the mother will be twice as old as her daughter. How old is the daughter now?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Define current ages.

D = daughter's age now.

M = mother's age now.

Step 2: Translate the current condition.

Mother is currently 3 times daughter:

$$M=3D$$

Step 3: Translate the future condition.

In 5 years, mother will be:

$$M+5$$

In 5 years, daughter will be:

$$D+5$$

Mother will be twice daughter:

$$M+5=2(D+5)$$

Step 4: Substitute M = 3D.

$$3D+5=2(D+5)$$

$$3D+5=2D+10$$

$$D=5$$

Answer: the daughter is 5 now.

Teacher note:
The parentheses are there because the entire future daughter age, D + 5, is doubled.

blocks.title: Ratio Problems
blocks.content:
When two quantities are in a ratio, use a multiplier.

If the ratio is 3:5, write the quantities as:

$$3k \text{ and } 5k$$

Tiny teaching example:

Two numbers are in the ratio 3:5. Their sum is 40.

Set up:

$$3k+5k=40$$

$$8k=40$$

$$k=5$$

Numbers:

$$3(5)=15$$

$$5(5)=25$$

Teacher note:
Ratio numbers are not always the final answers. They are usually the pattern.

blocks.title: Coin, Ticket, and Count/Value Problems
blocks.content:
These problems usually need two equations.

One equation counts items.

One equation counts value.

Tiny teaching example:

A jar has dimes and quarters totaling 40 coins and $7.00.

Let:

d = dimes

q = quarters

Count equation:

$$d+q=40$$

Value equation:

$$0.10d+0.25q=7.00$$

To avoid decimals, multiply by 100:

$$10d+25q=700$$

Teacher note:
If the problem gives number of items and total money, expect a count equation and a value equation.

blocks.title: Linear Cost Model
blocks.content:
Some problems have a fixed fee plus a cost per item.

Pattern:

$$C=F+rn$$

C = total cost.

F = fixed fee.

r = rate per unit.

n = number of units.

Tiny teaching example:

A catering order costs $50 plus $12 per guest. The budget is $170.

Equation:

$$50+12n=170$$

$$12n=120$$

$$n=10$$

Teacher note:
Fixed fee happens once. Rate per item repeats for every item.

blocks.title: Special Pattern: War Years / Consecutive Squares
blocks.content:
Some OAR-style questions use square years.

Tiny teaching example:

A war starts in year $x^2$ and ends in year $(x+1)^2$. The war lasted 29 years.

Duration means end minus start:

$$(x+1)^2-x^2=29$$

Expand:

$$x^2+2x+1-x^2=29$$

The $x^2$ terms cancel:

$$2x+1=29$$

$$x=14$$

Start year:

$$x^2=14^2=196$$

Teacher note:
The shortcut is $$(x+1)^2-x^2=2x+1$$.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Word to Equation
blocks.content:
Keep this at the end as a summary.

TRANSLATION DICTIONARY

is / equals:

$$=$$

sum / plus / increased by:

$$+$$

minus / difference / decreased by:

$$-$$

of / times / product:

$$\times$$

divided by / per / quotient:

$$\div$$

DANGEROUS PHRASES

“A less than B” means:

$$B-A$$

“A exceeds B by C” means:

$$A-B=C$$

AGE

in k years: add k to everyone.

k years ago: subtract k from everyone.

CONSECUTIVE NUMBERS

Integers:

$$n,n+1,n+2$$

Even or odd:

$$n,n+2,n+4$$

RATIO

Ratio a:b means:

$$ak,bk$$

LINEAR COST

$$C=F+rn$$

Teacher note:
Translate first. Solve second.

key_formulas: EXCEEDS BY: $$\text{A exceeds B by C} \Rightarrow A-B=C$$
LESS THAN: $$\text{A less than B} \Rightarrow B-A$$
AGE: in k years add k to everyone; k years ago subtract k from everyone.
common_mistakes: Reversing 'less than.' A less than B means B - A.
Reversing 'exceeds by.' A exceeds B by C means A - B = C.
Changing only one person's age in an age problem.
Guessing ratio numbers instead of using ak and bk.
Writing only the count equation and forgetting the value equation in coin/ticket problems.

conceptPages.title: Order of Operations
conceptPages.overview: Order of operations tells you what to do first when an expression has several operations. For OAR, the goal is to move through the expression in the correct order without inventing parentheses or doing operations out of order.

blocks.title: Start Here: Why Order Matters
blocks.content:
One expression can look simple but go wrong if you do the steps in the wrong order.

Example:

$$3+4\times2$$

Wrong path:

$$3+4=7,\quad 7\times2=14$$

Correct path:

Multiplication comes before addition.

$$4\times2=8$$

$$3+8=11$$

Teacher note:
Order of operations is not a new type of math. It is just the rule for which part to clean up first.

blocks.title: The Main Order: PEMDAS
blocks.content:
Use PEMDAS as your checklist.

P = Parentheses and grouping symbols first.

E = Exponents next.

M/D = Multiplication and division, left to right.

A/S = Addition and subtraction, left to right.

Tiny teaching example:

$$2^3+4(6-2)$$

Parentheses first:

$$6-2=4$$

Exponents next:

$$2^3=8$$

Multiplication next:

$$4(4)=16$$

Addition last:

$$8+16=24$$

Teacher note:
Do one layer at a time. Do not try to solve the whole expression in your head.

blocks.title: Practice — Basic PEMDAS
blocks.content:
Problem:
Evaluate.

$$5+3^2(4-1)$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Parentheses.

$$4-1=3$$

Now the expression is:

$$5+3^2(3)$$

Step 2: Exponents.

$$3^2=9$$

Now:

$$5+9(3)$$

Step 3: Multiplication.

$$9(3)=27$$

Step 4: Addition.

$$5+27=32$$

Answer: 32.

Teacher note:
The number next to parentheses means multiplication, but you still clean up the parentheses first.

blocks.title: Multiplication and Division Go Left to Right
blocks.content:
A huge trap is thinking multiplication always comes before division.

It does not.

Multiplication and division share the same level.

So you work from left to right.

Tiny teaching example:

$$12\div4\times3$$

The division comes first because it appears first from the left.

$$12\div4=3$$

Then multiply:

$$3\times3=9$$

Answer: 9.

Teacher note:
Do not rewrite this as $12\div(4\times3)$. That would add parentheses that were not there.

blocks.title: Practice — Left to Right
blocks.content:
Problem:
Evaluate.

$$18\div3\times2$$
blocks.solution:
Multiplication and division are tied.

Go left to right.

Step 1:

$$18\div3=6$$

Step 2:

$$6\times2=12$$

Answer: 12.

Teacher note:
Do not do $3\times2$ first unless parentheses tell you to.

blocks.title: Addition and Subtraction Also Go Left to Right
blocks.content:
Addition and subtraction also share the same level.

So you work left to right.

Tiny teaching example:

$$10-3+2$$

The subtraction comes first because it appears first.

$$10-3=7$$

Then add:

$$7+2=9$$

Teacher note:
Do not rewrite it as $10-(3+2)$. Again, that invents parentheses.

blocks.title: PEMDAS Still Applies Inside Parentheses
blocks.content:
Parentheses come first, but inside the parentheses you still follow PEMDAS.

Tiny teaching example:

$$(9-3^2)$$

Do not do $9-3$ first.

Inside the parentheses, exponents still come before subtraction.

$$3^2=9$$

So:

$$9-9=0$$

Teacher note:
Parentheses tell you where to work first. They do not cancel the order of operations inside them.

blocks.title: Practice — Nested Grouping
blocks.content:
Problem:
Evaluate.

$$3[2+(4\times3-5)]-8$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Start inside the inner parentheses.

$$4\times3-5$$

Multiplication first:

$$4\times3=12$$

Then subtract:

$$12-5=7$$

Step 2: Clean up the bracket.

$$[2+7]=9$$

Step 3: Multiply.

$$3(9)=27$$

Step 4: Subtract.

$$27-8=19$$

Answer: 19.

blocks.title: Exponent Belongs to Its Base
blocks.content:
An exponent applies to the base directly attached to it.

Tiny teaching example:

$$48\div4^2$$

The exponent belongs to 4 only.

So first:

$$4^2=16$$

Then:

$$48\div16=3$$

Teacher note:
This is not $(48\div4)^2$. Parentheses would have to be written for that.

blocks.title: Practice — Exponent in a Division Expression
blocks.content:
Problem:
Evaluate.

$$48\div4^2+3(8-5)$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Parentheses.

$$8-5=3$$

Step 2: Exponent.

$$4^2=16$$

Step 3: Division and multiplication, left to right.

$$48\div16=3$$

$$3(3)=9$$

Step 4: Addition.

$$3+9=12$$

Answer: 12.

Teacher note:
The exponent belongs to 4, not to $48\div4$.

blocks.title: Hard Pattern: Powers of the Same Base
blocks.content:
Sometimes order of operations combines with exponent rules.

If you see a sum of powers of the same base divided by another power, do not expand the huge powers.

Factor out the smallest power first.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\frac{2^5+2^6}{2^3}$$

Factor out $2^5$ from the top:

$$2^5(1+2)$$

So:

$$\frac{2^5(3)}{2^3}=2^{5-3}(3)=2^2(3)=12$$

Teacher note:
This is a harder pattern. The test is rewarding recognition, not giant arithmetic.

blocks.title: Common Traps
blocks.content:
Trap 1: Doing all multiplication before all division.

Correct: multiplication and division go left to right.

Trap 2: Doing all addition before subtraction.

Correct: addition and subtraction go left to right.

Trap 3: Applying an exponent to the wrong base.

In $48\div4^2$, square 4 first.

Trap 4: Ignoring PEMDAS inside parentheses.

In $(9-3^2)$, square 3 first.

Trap 5: Treating $4(5-3)$ as $4\times5-3$.

Correct: clean the parentheses first, then multiply.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Cheat Sheet — Order of Operations
blocks.content:
PEMDAS:

Parentheses / grouping symbols

Exponents

Multiplication and division left to right

Addition and subtraction left to right

Important notes:

M and D are tied. Go left to right.

A and S are tied. Go left to right.

PEMDAS still applies inside parentheses.

Exponent belongs to its base unless parentheses say otherwise.

For powers of same base:

$$a^m\cdot a^n=a^{m+n}$$

$$\frac{a^m}{a^n}=a^{m-n}$$

Teacher note:
This cheat sheet belongs at the end because the lesson taught the ideas first.

key_formulas: ORDER FLOW: Parentheses → Exponents → Multiplication/Division left to right → Addition/Subtraction left to right
EXPONENT BASE WARNING: in $$48\div4^2$$, compute $$4^2$$ first.
common_mistakes: Doing all multiplication before all division — they share equal priority and must be evaluated left to right.
Applying an exponent to the wrong base: in $48 \div 4^2$, compute $4^2 = 16$ first, then $48 \div 16$.
Ignoring PEMDAS inside parentheses: $(9 - 3^2)$ means evaluate $3^2$ first, giving $9 - 9 = 0$, not $(9-3)^2 = 36$.
Not treating a number directly next to parentheses as multiplication that must wait: $4(5-3) = 4 \times 2 = 8$.

conceptPages.title: Unit Conversions
conceptPages.overview: Unit conversions are partly problem-solving and partly memorization. This lesson teaches the minimum method you need for OAR-style questions: start with a real problem, decide whether to multiply or divide, cancel units when the problem gets more complex, and memorize the high-frequency facts so you can recall them quickly on test day.

blocks.title: Start Here: The Simple Version
blocks.content:
Do not overthink unit conversions at first. Most simple conversion problems ask you to change the label without changing the real amount.

Example: Convert 3.5 feet to inches.

You know:
$$1 \text{ ft} = 12 \text{ in}$$

So each foot contains 12 inches. Since you have 3.5 feet:
$$3.5 \times 12 = 42$$

Answer: 42 inches.

Tiny rule:
Bigger unit to smaller unit: usually multiply.

Smaller unit to bigger unit: usually divide.

Feet to inches is bigger to smaller, so multiply by 12.

blocks.title: Try It — Feet to Inches
blocks.content:
Problem: Convert 3.5 feet to inches.

Pause and try it before showing the solution.

Solution:
You know $1 \text{ ft} = 12 \text{ in}$.

Because feet are bigger than inches, multiply:
$$3.5 \times 12 = 42$$

Answer: 42 inches.

Quick mental check:
3 feet is 36 inches.
Half a foot is 6 inches.
$36 + 6 = 42$ inches.

This is enough for many simple OAR conversion questions.

blocks.title: Why the Fraction Method Works
blocks.content:
The official-looking method is called unit cancellation. It is useful when the problem has more than one conversion.

The only idea you need:
Put the unit you want to get rid of on the opposite side so it cancels.

For 3.5 feet to inches, you start with feet:
$$3.5 \text{ ft}$$

You want feet to disappear and inches to remain, so put feet on the bottom and inches on top:
$$3.5 \text{ ft} \times \frac{12 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ ft}}$$

The feet cancel:
$$\text{ft} \times \frac{\text{in}}{\text{ft}} = \text{in}$$

Then calculate:
$$3.5 \times 12 = 42$$

Answer: 42 inches.

If the unit cancels correctly, your setup is probably correct.

blocks.title: The One Setup Rule
blocks.content:
Ask yourself:

1. What unit do I have?
2. What unit do I want?
3. Where should the old unit go so it cancels?

If the old unit is currently on top, put it on the bottom of the conversion fraction.
If the old unit is currently on the bottom, put it on the top of the conversion fraction.

This matters most for speed problems like miles per hour to feet per second, because one unit is on top and one unit is on bottom.

blocks.title: Memorize These First
blocks.content:
Some conversions should be automatic. You do not need to understand them deeply every time; you need to recall them fast.

High priority:
$$12 \text{ in} = 1 \text{ ft}$$
$$3 \text{ ft} = 1 \text{ yd}$$
$$5{,}280 \text{ ft} = 1 \text{ mi}$$
$$16 \text{ oz} = 1 \text{ lb}$$
$$2{,}000 \text{ lb} = 1 \text{ ton}$$
$$4 \text{ qt} = 1 \text{ gal}$$
$$60 \text{ sec} = 1 \text{ min}$$
$$60 \text{ min} = 1 \text{ hr}$$
$$3{,}600 \text{ sec} = 1 \text{ hr}$$

Very useful shortcut:
$$60 \text{ mph} = 88 \text{ ft/s}$$

Area fact:
$$1 \text{ yd}^2 = 9 \text{ ft}^2$$

Do the flashcard drill for these. Your goal is to recall them fast enough to write them down at the start of the real test if needed.

blocks.title: Example 1 — mph to ft/s
blocks.content:
Problem: A vehicle travels at 60 miles per hour. What is this speed in feet per second?

This is a speed conversion, so you must convert BOTH parts:
miles to feet

hours to seconds

Start with:
$$60 \frac{\text{mi}}{\text{hr}}$$

Convert miles to feet:
$$1 \text{ mi} = 5{,}280 \text{ ft}$$

Convert hours to seconds:
$$1 \text{ hr} = 3{,}600 \text{ sec}$$

Your setup can be written as:
$$60 \times \frac{5{,}280}{3{,}600}$$

Calculate:
$$60 \times 5{,}280 = 316{,}800$$
$$316{,}800 \div 3{,}600 = 88$$

Answer: 88 ft/s.

This matches the shortcut:
$$60 \text{ mph} = 88 \text{ ft/s}$$

blocks.title: Your Method Is Correct
blocks.content:
If you wrote something like this:
$$\frac{60}{1} \times \frac{5{,}280}{3{,}600}$$

That is basically correct for 60 mph to feet per second.

Why it works:
60/1 means 60 miles per 1 hour.

5,280 changes miles to feet.

3,600 changes hours to seconds.

So the setup becomes:
$$60 \frac{\text{mi}}{\text{hr}} \times \frac{5{,}280 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ mi}} \times \frac{1 \text{ hr}}{3{,}600 \text{ sec}}$$

The miles cancel. The hours cancel. You are left with feet per second.

Use your compressed version when you understand the units. Use the full cancellation version when the problem feels confusing.

blocks.title: Example 2 — Speed Then Distance
blocks.content:
Problem: A vehicle travels at 45 mph. How many feet does it travel in 4 seconds?

This is a two-step problem.

Step 1: Convert 45 mph to ft/s.
$$45 \times \frac{5{,}280}{3{,}600} = 66 \text{ ft/s}$$

Shortcut using $60 \text{ mph} = 88 \text{ ft/s}$:
$$45 \text{ mph} = \frac{45}{60} \times 88$$
$$= \frac{3}{4} \times 88 = 66 \text{ ft/s}$$

Step 2: Use distance = rate times time.
$$D = R \times T$$
$$D = 66 \times 4 = 264$$

Answer: 264 feet.

Pattern to remember:
Convert speed first. Then multiply by time.

blocks.title: Example 3 — Area Conversion
blocks.content:
Problem: A floor measures 2 square yards. How many square feet is this?

This is not a normal yard-to-feet problem. It is area, so you must square the conversion.

You know:
$$1 \text{ yd} = 3 \text{ ft}$$

So:
$$1 \text{ yd}^2 = 3^2 \text{ ft}^2 = 9 \text{ ft}^2$$

Now convert:
$$2 \text{ yd}^2 \times 9 = 18 \text{ ft}^2$$

Answer: 18 square feet.

Big trap:
Do not say $2 \times 3 = 6$. That forgets that area has two dimensions.

Linear: $1 \text{ yd} = 3 \text{ ft}$
Area: $1 \text{ yd}^2 = 9 \text{ ft}^2$

blocks.title: Example 4 — Mixed Units
blocks.content:
Problem: A package weighs 6 pounds and 8 ounces. What is this weight entirely in ounces?

Mixed unit problems are usually easier than they look. Convert each part, then add.

Step 1: Convert pounds to ounces.
$$1 \text{ lb} = 16 \text{ oz}$$
$$6 \text{ lb} = 6 \times 16 = 96 \text{ oz}$$

Step 2: Add the extra 8 ounces.
$$96 + 8 = 104$$

Answer: 104 ounces.

Pattern to remember:
Convert the big unit part first. Then add the leftover smaller unit.
