$$1000^2=1{,}000{,}000=10^6$$

So log of that is 6.

Teacher note:
The power rule is often faster than expanding the full number.

blocks.title: Expanding Logs with Variables
blocks.content:
Some problems ask you to expand a log expression.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\log(x^2y)$$

First split multiplication:

$$\log(x^2)+\log(y)$$

Then move the exponent to the front:

$$2\log(x)+\log(y)$$

Teacher note:
For variables, use the same three rules: multiply becomes plus, divide becomes minus, exponent moves front.

blocks.title: Example 6 — Expand a Log Expression
blocks.content:
Problem:
Expand using log rules.

$$\log\left(\frac{x^3y^2}{z}\right)$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Use the quotient rule.

Division means subtract the denominator log.

$$\log\left(\frac{x^3y^2}{z}\right)=\log(x^3y^2)-\log(z)$$

Step 2: Use the product rule on the numerator.

$$\log(x^3y^2)=\log(x^3)+\log(y^2)$$

So:

$$\log(x^3)+\log(y^2)-\log(z)$$

Step 3: Use the power rule.

$$3\log(x)+2\log(y)-\log(z)$$

Answer:

$$3\log(x)+2\log(y)-\log(z)$$

Teacher note:
Do not turn x³ into 3x. The exponent moves in front of the log.

blocks.title: Same-Base Logs: Set the Insides Equal
blocks.content:
If two logs have the same base and are equal, the inside parts must be equal.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\log_2(x)=\log_2(8)$$

Same log base on both sides means:

$$x=8$$

Another example:

$$\log_3(x+1)=\log_3(9)$$

Set the insides equal:

$$x+1=9$$

$$x=8$$

Teacher note:
This only works when the log bases match.

blocks.title: Example 7 — Same-Base Log Equation
blocks.content:
Problem:
Solve for x.

$$2\log_4(x)=\log_4(36)$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Use the power rule on the left side.

$$2\log_4(x)=\log_4(x^2)$$

Step 2: Rewrite the equation.

$$\log_4(x^2)=\log_4(36)$$

Step 3: Same base logs are equal, so set the insides equal.

$$x^2=36$$

Step 4: Solve.

$$x=6$$

Answer: x = 6.

Teacher note:
Technically x² = 36 has ±6, but logs require x to be positive here because log₄(x) was in the original problem. So use x = 6.

blocks.title: Solving for the Base
blocks.content:
Sometimes the unknown is the base of the log.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\log_x(16)=2$$

Translate it:

$$x^2=16$$

So:

$$x=4$$

For logs, the base must be positive and cannot be 1, so use the positive answer.

Teacher note:
When the base is x, still use the same conversion: log form becomes exponent form.

blocks.title: Example 8 — Solve for the Base
blocks.content:
Problem:
Solve for x.

$$\log_x(\sqrt{2})=\frac{1}{6}$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Convert to exponent form.

$$\log_x(\sqrt{2})=\frac{1}{6}$$

means:

$$x^{1/6}=\sqrt{2}$$

Step 2: Rewrite the square root.

$$\sqrt{2}=2^{1/2}$$

So:

$$x^{1/6}=2^{1/2}$$

Step 3: Raise both sides to the 6th power.

$$x=(2^{1/2})^6$$

Step 4: Multiply exponents.

$$x=2^{6/2}=2^3=8$$

Answer: x = 8.

Teacher note:
This looks strange, but it is still just log-to-exponent conversion.

blocks.title: Change of Base: Calculator Backup Formula
blocks.content:
Change of base lets you rewrite a log using a different base.

Formula:

$$\log_b(x)=\frac{\log(x)}{\log(b)}$$

or:

$$\log_b(x)=\frac{\ln(x)}{\ln(b)}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$\log_5(125)=\frac{\log(125)}{\log(5)}$$

But for OAR-style questions, it is often faster to ask:

5 to what power equals 125?

$$5^3=125$$

So:

$$\log_5(125)=3$$

Teacher note:
Know the formula, but use power recognition first when the numbers are simple.

blocks.title: Logs Can Hide Factoring
blocks.content:
Some harder log problems secretly test factoring.

Tiny teaching example:

$$\log(x^2-4)-\log(x-2)=1$$

First use the quotient rule:

$$\log\left(\frac{x^2-4}{x-2}\right)=1$$

Then factor:

$$x^2-4=(x+2)(x-2)$$

Cancel:

$$\frac{(x+2)(x-2)}{x-2}=x+2$$

So:

$$\log(x+2)=1$$

Convert to exponent form:

$$x+2=10^1=10$$

$$x=8$$

Teacher note:
If you see x² inside a log, check whether factoring will simplify it.

blocks.title: Example 9 — Log Problem with Factoring
blocks.content:
Problem:
Solve for x.

$$\log(x^2-9)-\log(x-3)=1$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Use the quotient rule.

$$\log(x^2-9)-\log(x-3)=\log\left(\frac{x^2-9}{x-3}\right)$$

So the equation becomes:

$$\log\left(\frac{x^2-9}{x-3}\right)=1$$

Step 2: Factor the numerator.

$$x^2-9=(x+3)(x-3)$$

Step 3: Cancel x - 3.

$$\frac{(x+3)(x-3)}{x-3}=x+3$$

So:

$$\log(x+3)=1$$

Step 4: Convert to exponent form.

No base is written, so base 10.

$$x+3=10^1$$

$$x+3=10$$

$$x=7$$

Answer: x = 7.

Teacher note:
This is why factoring still matters. Logs sometimes hide a factorable expression.

blocks.title: Common Traps
blocks.content:
Trap 1: Forgetting that logs are exponent questions backwards.

Trap 2: Thinking log(1000) means 1000 divided by 10. It does not. It asks 10 to what power equals 1000.

Trap 3: Adding inside numbers when logs are added.

$$\log(4)+\log(25)$$

is not log(29). It is log(100).

Trap 4: Subtracting inside numbers when logs are subtracted.

$$\log(1000)-\log(10)$$

is not log(990). It is log(100).

Trap 5: Forgetting that log inputs must be positive.

Trap 6: Missing factoring inside log equations.

blocks.title: What to Remember
blocks.content:
Main conversion:

$$\log_b(x)=y \iff b^y=x$$

Common logs:

$$\log(1)=0,\ \log(10)=1,\ \log(100)=2,\ \log(1000)=3$$

Rules:

Plus between logs means multiply inside.

Minus between logs means divide inside.

Exponent inside a log moves to the front.

Same-base logs equal each other means set the insides equal.

If the base is unknown, convert to exponent form.

If x² appears inside a log, check for factoring.

common_mistakes: Forgetting that a logarithm is an exponent question backwards.
Adding or subtracting the numbers inside logs instead of using product/quotient rules.
Using log rules with different bases without changing base first.
Keeping negative solutions when the original log input must be positive.
Missing factoring inside log equations.
Overusing change of base when simple power recognition is faster.

conceptPages.title: Series & Summation
conceptPages.overview: This lesson teaches series and summation from the ground up. The goal is not to learn advanced math. The goal is to recognize what the test is asking: find the next number, find a specific term, or add a list of numbers quickly.

blocks.title: First: What Is This Topic Even Asking?
blocks.content:
Series and summation questions are about lists of numbers.

The test usually asks one of three things:

1. What number comes next?

Example:

$$3, 6, 9, 12, \_$$

2. What is a certain term?

Example:

What is the 10th number in the pattern?

3. What is the total if you add the numbers?

Example:

$$1+2+3+\cdots+100$$

Teacher note:
Before using any formula, ask: Am I finding a number in the list, or am I adding the list?

blocks.title: Sequence Means List
blocks.content:
A sequence is just a list of numbers in order.

Example:

$$3, 7, 11, 15, \ldots$$

Each number in the list is called a term.

3 is the 1st term.

7 is the 2nd term.

11 is the 3rd term.

15 is the 4th term.

If the question asks for the next term or the 5th term, you are working with a sequence.

Teacher note:
Sequence = list. Term = one number in the list.

blocks.title: Series Means Add the List
blocks.content:
A series means you are adding the numbers in a sequence.

Sequence:

$$3, 7, 11, 15$$

Series:

$$3+7+11+15$$

The word sum means the total after adding.

Example:

$$3+7+11+15=36$$

So the sum is 36.

Teacher note:
Sum just means total. If a question asks for a sum, it wants you to add.

blocks.title: Two Main Patterns: Add Pattern or Multiply Pattern
blocks.content:
Most OAR series questions start with one question:

Is the pattern adding the same amount or multiplying by the same amount?

Add pattern:

$$3, 7, 11, 15, \ldots$$

Each number increases by 4.

This is called arithmetic.

Multiply pattern:

$$2, 6, 18, 54, \ldots$$

Each number is multiplied by 3.

This is called geometric.

Teacher note:
The names sound academic, but the test-day idea is simple: arithmetic = add/subtract. geometric = multiply/divide.

blocks.title: Arithmetic Sequence: Finding a Number in the List
blocks.content:
Use this when the pattern adds the same amount each time.

Tiny teaching example:

$$3, 7, 11, 15, \ldots$$

Each term increases by 4.

So the next term is:

$$15+4=19$$

If the question asks for a far-away term, use:

$$a_n=a_1+(n-1)d$$

What the letters mean:

$a_n$ = the term you want

$a_1$ = the first term

$n$ = the term number

$d$ = the amount being added each time

Teacher note:
The formula is just a shortcut for repeated adding.

blocks.title: Practice — Arithmetic nth Term
blocks.content:
Problem:
What is the 5th term of the arithmetic sequence?

$$4, 10, 16, 22, \ldots$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Find the amount being added.

$$10-4=6$$

So d = 6.

Step 2: Since this asks for the 5th term, you can list one more term.

1st term: 4

2nd term: 10

3rd term: 16

4th term: 22

5th term:

$$22+6=28$$

Answer: 28.

Teacher note:
For small term numbers, listing is often easier than using the formula.

blocks.title: Arithmetic Sum: Adding a List Fast
blocks.content:
Use this when the pattern adds the same amount and the question asks for the total.

Sum means total after adding.

Main formula:

$$S_n=\text{count}\times\frac{\text{first}+\text{last}}{2}$$

This means:

1. Count how many numbers are in the list.

2. Find the average of the first and last number.

3. Multiply count by that average.

Tiny teaching example:

Find the sum from 1 to 5.

First = 1.

Last = 5.

Common difference d = 1.

Count:

$$\frac{5-1}{1}+1=5$$

Average of first and last:

$$\frac{1+5}{2}=3$$

Sum:

$$5\times3=15$$

So:

$$1+2+3+4+5=15$$

Teacher note:
This is the main arithmetic sum method. Use it for evenly spaced lists like 1,2,3,... or 3,6,9,...

blocks.title: Counting Terms: Use Step Size
blocks.content:
If the question asks for the sum from one number to another, you need to know how many terms there are.

Use the full count formula:

$$\text{count}=\frac{\text{last}-\text{first}}{\text{common difference d}}+1$$

Common difference d means how much the list increases by each time.

Tiny teaching example 1:

How many numbers are from 3 to 7?

List:

3, 4, 5, 6, 7

Common difference d = 1.

Formula:

$$\frac{7-3}{1}+1=5$$

There are 5 numbers.

Tiny teaching example 2:

How many numbers are in this list?

$$3,6,9,12,\ldots,30$$

First = 3.

Last = 30.

Common difference d = 3.

Formula:

$$\frac{30-3}{3}+1=\frac{27}{3}+1=10$$

There are 10 numbers.

Teacher note:
The shortcut last − first + 1 only works when the common difference d is 1. If the list jumps by 2, 3, 4, etc., use the full count formula.

blocks.title: Practice — Sum From First to Last
blocks.content:
Problem:
Find the sum of all integers from 2 to 223.
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Understand what sum means.

The question wants the total:

$$2+3+4+5+\cdots+223$$

Step 2: Identify first, last, and common difference d.

First = 2.

Last = 223.

Common difference d = 1.

Step 3: Count how many numbers are being added.

Use:

$$\text{count}=\frac{\text{last}-\text{first}}{\text{common difference d}}+1$$

So:

$$\text{count}=\frac{223-2}{1}+1=221+1=222$$

There are 222 numbers.

Step 4: Find the average of the first and last number.

$$\frac{2+223}{2}=\frac{225}{2}=112.5$$

Step 5: Multiply count by average.

$$222\times112.5=24{,}975$$

Answer: 24,975.

Teacher note:
Because this list increases by 1, last − first + 1 works. For a list like 3, 6, 9, ..., 30, divide by the common difference d first.

blocks.title: Sum From 1 to n Shortcut
blocks.content:
A common shortcut is:

$$1+2+3+\cdots+n=\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$

This is just the arithmetic sum rule for numbers starting at 1.

Tiny teaching example:

Find:

$$1+2+3+\cdots+10$$

Use the shortcut:

$$\frac{10(10+1)}{2}=\frac{10(11)}{2}=55$$

Teacher note:
If the list starts at 1 and goes to n, use this shortcut.

blocks.title: Practice — Find n From a Sum
blocks.content:
Problem:
What is the smallest value of n such that:

$$1+2+3+\cdots+n=210$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Use the shortcut for sum from 1 to n.

$$\frac{n(n+1)}{2}=210$$

Step 2: Multiply both sides by 2.

$$n(n+1)=420$$

Step 3: Find two consecutive numbers that multiply to 420.

Try 20 and 21:

$$20\times21=420$$

So:

$$n=20$$

Answer: n = 20.

Teacher note:
This question asks where the sum reaches 210. It is not asking you to add the numbers one by one.

blocks.title: Geometric Sequence: Multiply Pattern
blocks.content:
Use this when the pattern multiplies by the same number each time.

Tiny teaching example:

$$2, 6, 18, 54, \ldots$$

Each term is multiplied by 3.

So the next term is:

$$54\times3=162$$

This is called a geometric sequence.

For a far-away term, use:

$$a_n=a_1r^{n-1}$$

What the letters mean:

$a_1$ = first term

$r$ = common ratio, or what you multiply by each time

$n$ = term number

Teacher note:
Geometric = multiply by the same number.

blocks.title: Practice — Geometric nth Term
blocks.content:
Problem:
What is the 5th term of the geometric sequence?

$$3, 6, 12, 24, \ldots$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Find what the sequence multiplies by.

$$6\div3=2$$

So r = 2.

Step 2: Since this asks for the 5th term, list one more term.

1st term: 3

2nd term: 6

3rd term: 12

4th term: 24

5th term:

$$24\times2=48$$

Answer: 48.

Teacher note:
For small term numbers, listing is easier than the formula.

blocks.title: Geometric Sum: Add a Multiply Pattern
blocks.content:
A geometric sum means you are adding a list that multiplies by the same number.

Example:

$$1+2+4+8+16+32$$

This is not asking for the next term.

It asks for the total.

You can use the formula:

$$S_n=\frac{a_1(r^n-1)}{r-1}$$

But on the OAR, if there are only a few terms, listing and adding may be easier.

Tiny teaching example:

Sum the first 4 terms of:

$$1,2,4,8,\ldots$$

Add:

$$1+2+4+8=15$$

Formula gives the same result:

$$\frac{1(2^4-1)}{2-1}=15$$

Teacher note:
Do not confuse the next power with the sum. $2^6=64$, but $1+2+4+8+16+32=63$.

blocks.title: Practice — Geometric Sum
blocks.content:
Problem:
What is the sum of the first 6 terms of the geometric series with $a_1=1$ and $r=2$?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Write out the first 6 terms.

Start with 1 and multiply by 2 each time:

1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32

Step 2: Add them.

$$1+2+4+8+16+32=63$$

Answer: 63.

Teacher note:
The tempting wrong answer is 64, because $2^6=64$. But the question asks for the sum of the first 6 terms, not the next power.

blocks.title: Infinite Geometric Series: Only If It Shrinks
blocks.content:
Infinite means the pattern keeps going forever.

An infinite sum sounds impossible, but sometimes the terms get smaller and smaller.

Example:

$$8+4+2+1+\cdots$$

The terms keep getting cut in half.

The total gets closer and closer to a final number.

Formula:

$$S_{\infty}=\frac{a_1}{1-r}$$

Only use this if:

$$|r|<1$$

That means the ratio is between -1 and 1.

Teacher note:
If the terms are shrinking, the infinite series may have a finite total. If the terms are growing, it does not settle.

blocks.title: Practice — Infinite Geometric Sum
blocks.content:
Problem:
What is the sum of the infinite geometric series?

$$8+4+2+1+\cdots$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Find the first term.

$$a_1=8$$

Step 2: Find what you multiply by each time.

$$4\div8=\frac{1}{2}$$

So r = 1/2.

Step 3: Since 1/2 is less than 1, the series has a finite sum.

Step 4: Use the formula.

$$S_{\infty}=\frac{a_1}{1-r}$$

$$S_{\infty}=\frac{8}{1-1/2}$$

$$S_{\infty}=\frac{8}{1/2}=16$$

Answer: 16.

Teacher note:
The sums go 8, 12, 14, 15, 15.5... They get closer and closer to 16.

blocks.title: Sigma Notation Means Plug In and Add
blocks.content:
Sigma is this symbol:

$$\sum$$

It means add.

Example:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3}(2i+1)$$

This means plug in i = 1, 2, and 3.

For i = 1:

$$2(1)+1=3$$

For i = 2:

$$2(2)+1=5$$

For i = 3:

$$2(3)+1=7$$

Now add:

$$3+5+7=15$$

Teacher note:
If sigma notation looks scary, ignore the symbol and list the terms.

blocks.title: Practice — Sigma Notation
blocks.content:
Problem:
Evaluate:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{7}(2i+1)$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Plug in i = 1 through 7.

The terms are:

$$3,5,7,9,11,13,15$$

Step 2: Add the terms.

$$3+5+7+9+11+13+15=63$$

Answer: 63.

Teacher note:
There are only 7 terms, so listing them is the easiest method.

blocks.title: Special Shortcut: First n Odd Numbers
blocks.content:
The sum of the first n positive odd numbers is:

$$n^2$$

Example:

First 4 odd numbers:

$$1+3+5+7=16$$

And:

$$4^2=16$$

So if the question asks for the sum of the first 10 odd numbers:

$$10^2=100$$

Teacher note:
This is a shortcut worth memorizing.

blocks.title: Weird Pattern Questions
blocks.content:
Not every sequence is arithmetic or geometric.

If adding the same number does not work, and multiplying the same number does not work, try a square pattern.

Tiny teaching example:

$$1,2,5,26,\_$$

Check each step:

$$1^2+1=2$$

$$2^2+1=5$$

$$5^2+1=26$$

So the next term is:

$$26^2+1=677$$

Teacher note:
For strange OAR patterns, try previous number squared plus or minus something.

blocks.title: Common Traps
blocks.content:
Trap 1: Not knowing whether the question asks for a term or a sum.

Term = one number in the list.

Sum = total after adding.

Trap 2: Forgetting +1 when counting terms.

From 2 to 223:

$$223-2+1=222$$

Trap 3: Mixing up arithmetic and geometric.

Arithmetic adds.

Geometric multiplies.

Trap 4: Picking the next power instead of the total.

For $1+2+4+8+16+32$, the sum is 63, not 64.

Trap 5: Panicking at sigma notation.

Sigma just means plug in and add.

blocks.title: Test-Day Decision Guide
blocks.content:
Step 1: Ask what the question wants.

Next number or specific term? Use sequence thinking.

Total after adding? Use series/sum thinking.

Step 2: Identify the pattern.

Adding same amount? Arithmetic.

Multiplying same amount? Geometric.

Weird pattern? Try previous squared plus or minus something.

Step 3: Use the simplest method.

Small list? List it out.

Long arithmetic sum? Use count × average.

Sigma? Plug in and add.

Infinite geometric? Only use formula if the terms shrink.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Categorized
blocks.content:
Write this as a categorized scratch-paper guide.

ARITHMETIC = plus / minus same amount.

Use $d$.

$d$ = common difference.

1. Arithmetic — find one number / nth term.

Use when the question asks:

What is the 5th term?

What is the 10th term?

Which term equals 65?

Formula:

$$a_n=a_1+(n-1)d$$

2. Arithmetic — find sum / total.

Use when the question asks:

Find the sum.

Find the total.

Add all integers from first to last.

Formula:

$$S=\text{count}\times\frac{\text{first}+\text{last}}{2}$$

3. Arithmetic — find count for the sum.

Use this before the arithmetic sum formula.

Formula:

$$\text{count}=\frac{\text{last}-\text{first}}{d}+1$$

Special arithmetic shortcut:

$$1+2+\cdots+n=\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$

Odd number shortcut:

$$1+3+5+\cdots+(2n-1)=n^2$$

GEOMETRIC = times / divide same amount.

Use $r$.

$r$ = common ratio.

4. Geometric — find one number / nth term.

Use when the list multiplies or divides by the same amount and asks for a term.

Formula:

$$a_n=a_1r^{n-1}$$

5. Geometric — find finite sum / total of first n terms.

Use when the list multiplies or divides by the same amount and asks for the sum of a limited number of terms.

Formula:

$$S_n=\frac{a_1(r^n-1)}{r-1}$$

6. Geometric — find infinite sum / total forever.

Use only when the terms shrink and $|r|<1$.

Formula:

$$S_{\infty}=\frac{a_1}{1-r}$$

SIGMA:

$$\sum$$

means plug in each value and add.

WEIRD PATTERN:

Try these in order:

add/subtract same amount

multiply/divide same amount

previous number squared

previous number squared plus 1

Teacher note:
Nothing here is optional in the sense of “ignore it forever.” The category tells you when to use it. Arithmetic formulas are for plus/minus patterns. Geometric formulas are for times/divide patterns.

key_formulas: ARITHMETIC = plus/minus same amount; use d = common difference.
ARITHMETIC — find one number / nth term: $$a_n=a_1+(n-1)d$$
ARITHMETIC SHORTCUT — sum from 1 to n: $$1+2+\cdots+n=\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$
ARITHMETIC SHORTCUT — first n odd numbers: $$1+3+5+\cdots+(2n-1)=n^2$$
GEOMETRIC = times/divide same amount; use r = common ratio.
GEOMETRIC — find one number / nth term: $$a_n=a_1r^{n-1}$$
GEOMETRIC — find finite sum / total of first n terms: $$S_n=\frac{a_1(r^n-1)}{r-1}$$
GEOMETRIC — find infinite sum / total forever, only when |r|<1: $$S_{\infty}=\frac{a_1}{1-r}$$
SIGMA — $$\sum$$ means plug in each value and add.
WEIRD PATTERN — try add/subtract, multiply/divide, previous squared, previous squared plus 1.
common_mistakes: Not knowing whether the question asks for one term or a total sum.
Forgetting that sum means total after adding.
Forgetting plus 1 when counting terms from a first number to a last number.
Using arithmetic formulas on geometric sequences.
Using geometric formulas on arithmetic sequences.
Picking the next power instead of the sum of the terms.
Trying to use the infinite series formula when the ratio is not smaller than 1 in absolute value.
Overcomplicating sigma notation instead of listing the terms.

conceptPages.title: Quadratic Equations
conceptPages.overview: Quadratic equations are algebra problems with x². The goal for OAR is not to master every algebra method equally. The goal is to recognize the form, choose the fastest solving method, and avoid common traps like forgetting ±, reading factor signs wrong, or using the quadratic formula before trying an easier method.

blocks.title: Start Here: What Is a Quadratic?
blocks.content:
A quadratic equation usually looks like this:

$$ax^2+bx+c=0$$

The important part is the $x^2$.

Examples:

$$x^2-5x+6=0$$

$$2x^2+3x-2=0$$

$$3x^2-12=0$$

The main test question is usually:

Solve for x.

Teacher note:
For quadratics, the first thing to check is whether the equation is already equal to 0. Factoring only works cleanly when one side is 0.

blocks.title: Decision Flow: Which Method Should I Use?
blocks.content:
Use this order on the test.

1. If there is no middle x term, isolate $x^2$.

Example:

$$3x^2-12=0$$

Move and divide until you get:

$$x^2=4$$

Then:

$$x=\pm2$$

2. If it looks easy to factor, factor it.

Example:

$$x^2-5x+6=0$$

Factors:

$$(x-2)(x-3)=0$$

3. If there is a number in front of $x^2$, try AC method or use the quadratic formula.

4. If factoring feels bad, use the quadratic formula.

Teacher note:
Do not start with the hardest method. Try the simple pattern first.

blocks.title: Method 1: Factoring When x² Has No Coefficient
blocks.content:
For this type:

$$x^2+bx+c=0$$

Ask:

What two numbers multiply to c and add to b?

Tiny teaching example:

$$x^2-5x+6=0$$

You need two numbers that multiply to 6 and add to -5.

Those numbers are -2 and -3.

So:

$$x^2-5x+6=(x-2)(x-3)$$

Then set each factor equal to 0:

$$x-2=0 \Rightarrow x=2$$

$$x-3=0 \Rightarrow x=3$$

Teacher note:
The signs flip when solving. $(x-2)$ gives $x=2$, not $x=-2$.

blocks.title: Practice — Solve by Factoring
blocks.content:
Problem:
Solve.

$$x^2-2x-15=0$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Find two numbers that multiply to -15 and add to -2.

Because the product is negative, one number is positive and one is negative.

The pair is -5 and +3:

$$(-5)(3)=-15$$

$$-5+3=-2$$

Step 2: Factor.

$$x^2-2x-15=(x-5)(x+3)$$

Step 3: Set each factor equal to 0.

$$x-5=0 \Rightarrow x=5$$

$$x+3=0 \Rightarrow x=-3$$

Answer: x = 5 or x = -3.

Teacher note:
For one positive and one negative factor, the bigger number controls the sign of the middle term.

blocks.title: Method 2: No Middle Term
blocks.content:
Sometimes the quadratic has $x^2$ but no regular x term.

Example:

$$3x^2-12=0$$

Do not factor if you do not need to.

Just isolate $x^2$.

$$3x^2=12$$

$$x^2=4$$

Then take the square root:

$$x=\pm2$$

Teacher note:
When $x^2=\text{positive number}$, remember both positive and negative answers.

blocks.title: Practice — No Middle Term
blocks.content:
Problem:
Solve.

$$4x^2-36=0$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Add 36 to both sides.

$$4x^2=36$$

Step 2: Divide by 4.

$$x^2=9$$

Step 3: Take the square root.

$$x=\pm3$$

Answer: x = 3 or x = -3.

Teacher note:
Do not answer only 3. Both 3 and -3 work because both square to 9.

blocks.title: Method 3: Quadratic Formula
blocks.content:
The quadratic formula works for any quadratic equation:

$$x=\frac{-b\pm\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}$$

Use it when factoring is not obvious.

For:

$$ax^2+bx+c=0$$

$a$ is the number in front of $x^2$.

$b$ is the number in front of x.

$c$ is the constant.

Tiny teaching example:

$$2x^2+3x-2=0$$

$a=2$, $b=3$, $c=-2$.

Teacher note:
Most mistakes happen from copying a, b, or c wrong. Include the negative sign if c or b is negative.

blocks.title: Practice — Quadratic Formula
blocks.content:
Problem:
Solve using the quadratic formula.

$$2x^2+3x-2=0$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Identify a, b, and c.

$$a=2$$

$$b=3$$

$$c=-2$$

Step 2: Use the formula.

$$x=\frac{-b\pm\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}$$

Step 3: Substitute.

$$x=\frac{-3\pm\sqrt{3^2-4(2)(-2)}}{2(2)}$$

Step 4: Simplify inside the square root.

$$3^2-4(2)(-2)=9+16=25$$

So:

$$x=\frac{-3\pm5}{4}$$

Step 5: Split into two answers.

$$x=\frac{-3+5}{4}=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2}$$

$$x=\frac{-3-5}{4}=\frac{-8}{4}=-2$$

Answer: x = 1/2 or x = -2.

Teacher note:
The ± means you must do both plus and minus.

blocks.title: Discriminant: How Many Real Answers?
blocks.content:
The discriminant is the part under the square root in the quadratic formula:

$$b^2-4ac$$

It tells you how many real solutions there are.

If it is positive:

Two real solutions.

If it is zero:

One repeated real solution.

If it is negative:

No real solutions.

Tiny teaching example:

$$x^2+4x+7=0$$

$a=1$, $b=4$, $c=7$.

$$b^2-4ac=4^2-4(1)(7)=16-28=-12$$

Since it is negative, there are no real solutions.

Teacher note:
If the question only asks how many real solutions, you do not need to solve the whole equation.

blocks.title: Repeated Root / Perfect Square
blocks.content:
If the discriminant is 0, the quadratic has one repeated solution.

Example:

$$4x^2-4x+1=0$$

This factors as:

$$(2x-1)^2=0$$

So:

$$2x-1=0$$

$$x=\frac{1}{2}$$

There is only one answer, repeated twice.

Teacher note:
Do not write ±1/2 here. The factor is $(2x-1)^2$, so the only solution is $x=1/2$.

blocks.title: Vertex: Maximum or Minimum Point
blocks.content:
The vertex is the turning point of a parabola.

For:

$$y=ax^2+bx+c$$

The x-value of the vertex is:

$$x=\frac{-b}{2a}$$

Then plug that x-value back into the equation to find y.

Tiny teaching example:

$$y=x^2-4x+1$$

$a=1$, $b=-4$.

$$x=\frac{-(-4)}{2(1)}=2$$

Plug in x = 2:

$$y=2^2-4(2)+1=4-8+1=-3$$

Vertex:

$$(2,-3)$$

Teacher note:
For projectile problems, the vertex gives the maximum height.

blocks.title: Practice — Projectile Maximum
blocks.content:
Problem:
A projectile's height is given by:

$$h(t)=-16t^2+64t$$

What is the maximum height?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Maximum height happens at the vertex.

Use:

$$t=\frac{-b}{2a}$$

Step 2: Identify a and b.

$$a=-16$$

$$b=64$$

Step 3: Find the time at the vertex.

$$t=\frac{-64}{2(-16)}=\frac{-64}{-32}=2$$

Step 4: Plug t = 2 into the height equation.

$$h(2)=-16(2^2)+64(2)$$

$$h(2)=-16(4)+128$$

$$h(2)=-64+128=64$$

Answer: 64 feet.

Teacher note:
The vertex gives the maximum because the coefficient of $t^2$ is negative, so the parabola opens downward.

blocks.title: Vieta's Formulas: Sum and Product of Roots
blocks.content:
Sometimes the question asks for the sum or product of roots without asking you to solve.

For:

$$ax^2+bx+c=0$$

Sum of roots:

$$-\frac{b}{a}$$

Product of roots:

$$\frac{c}{a}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$x^2-7x+12=0$$

$a=1$, $b=-7$, $c=12$.

Sum of roots:

$$-\frac{-7}{1}=7$$

Product of roots:

$$\frac{12}{1}=12$$

Teacher note:
This is faster than solving when the question only asks for sum or product.

blocks.title: Completing the Square: Lower Priority
blocks.content:
Completing the square is a method for solving quadratics, but for OAR test strategy, it is lower priority than factoring and the quadratic formula.

Basic move:

For:

$$x^2+bx=k$$

add:

$$\left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^2$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$x^2-6x+5=0$$

Move 5:

$$x^2-6x=-5$$

Add:

$$\left(\frac{-6}{2}\right)^2=9$$

So:

$$x^2-6x+9=4$$

$$(x-3)^2=4$$

$$x-3=\pm2$$

$$x=5 \text{ or } x=1$$

Teacher note:
If a practice question specifically says “complete the square,” use this method. Otherwise, factoring or the formula is usually faster.

blocks.title: Word Problems: Translate First
blocks.content:
Quadratic word problems usually come from area or projectile motion.

For area:

1. Define the unknown.

2. Write the relationship.

3. Set up the equation.

4. Solve.

Example:
A rectangle has width w and length w + 3.

Area is 28.

$$w(w+3)=28$$

$$w^2+3w-28=0$$

Then solve by factoring.

Teacher note:
Reject negative lengths in geometry word problems.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Quadratics
blocks.content:
Write this as your scratch-paper guide.

QUADRATIC FORM:

$$ax^2+bx+c=0$$

METHOD FLOW:

No middle x term:

Isolate $x^2$, then use:

$$x=\pm\sqrt{k}$$

Easy factoring:

Find two numbers that multiply to c and add to b.

Then use zero product:

$$(x-p)(x-q)=0 \Rightarrow x=p \text{ or } x=q$$

Hard factoring / not obvious:

Use quadratic formula:

$$x=\frac{-b\pm\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}$$

HOW MANY REAL SOLUTIONS:

Discriminant:

$$D=b^2-4ac$$

$$D>0: 2 \text{ real solutions}$$

$$D=0: 1 \text{ repeated real solution}$$

$$D<0: \text{ no real solutions}$$

VERTEX / MAX OR MIN:

$$x=\frac{-b}{2a}$$

Then plug x back in to find y.

ROOT SUM / PRODUCT:

Sum:

$$-\frac{b}{a}$$

Product:

$$\frac{c}{a}$$

LOWER PRIORITY:

Completing the square:

Move constant, add $(b/2)^2$ to both sides.

Teacher note:
For your first pass, prioritize factoring, no-middle-term solving, quadratic formula, discriminant, and vertex.

key_formulas: ZERO PRODUCT PROPERTY: $$(A)(B)=0 \Rightarrow A=0 \text{ or } B=0$$
DISCRIMINANT MEANING: $$D>0:2\text{ real};\quad D=0:1\text{ repeated};\quad D<0:0\text{ real}$$
COMPLETING THE SQUARE: $$x^2+bx=k \Rightarrow \text{add }\left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^2\text{ to both sides}$$
common_mistakes: Forgetting to set the equation equal to zero before factoring.
Reading factor signs directly instead of solving each factor.
Forgetting ± when taking the square root or using the quadratic formula.
Copying a, b, or c incorrectly, especially when b or c is negative.
Thinking a negative discriminant means the equation has no solutions at all. It means no real solutions.
Keeping a negative length in a geometry word problem.
Using completing the square when the problem does not require it and an easier method works.

conceptPages.title: Systems of Equations
conceptPages.overview: Systems of equations are problems where more than one equation must be true at the same time. This version teaches each pattern first, then gives a practice problem that uses the same exact strategy. Harder patterns are included, but the scratch-paper summary stays at the end.

blocks.title: Start Here: What Is a System?
blocks.content:
A system of equations means more than one equation must be true at the same time.

Example:

$$x+y=10$$

$$x-y=4$$

The answer is not just one number.

The answer is the pair that works in both equations.

For this example, the answer is:

$$x=7,\ y=3$$

because:

$$7+3=10$$

and:

$$7-3=4$$

Teacher note:
Systems are about finding values that satisfy all equations at the same time.

blocks.title: Before Solving: Choose the Method
blocks.content:
There are two main methods.

Elimination means cancel one variable.

Substitution means replace one variable with what it equals.

Use elimination when the equations are lined up and you can make one variable disappear.

Use substitution when one variable is already alone, or easy to get alone.

Teacher note:
Do not memorize elimination and substitution as random math words. Elimination = cancel. Substitution = plug in.

blocks.title: Elimination Level 1: Something Already Cancels
blocks.content:
Use this when one variable already has opposite signs.

Tiny teaching example:

$$x+y=9$$

$$x-y=1$$

The y terms are:

$$+y$$

and:

$$-y$$

So they cancel when you add the equations.

Add straight down:

$$x+x=2x$$

$$y+(-y)=0$$

$$9+1=10$$

So:

$$2x=10$$

$$x=5$$

Now plug back in:

$$5+y=9$$

$$y=4$$

Answer:

$$x=5,\ y=4$$

Teacher note:
This is the easiest elimination case because nothing needs to be multiplied first.

blocks.title: Practice — Elimination When Something Already Cancels
blocks.content:
Problem:
Solve the system.

$$x+y=10$$

$$x-y=4$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Notice that +y and -y already cancel.

Step 2: Add the equations.

$$x+x=2x$$

$$y+(-y)=0$$

$$10+4=14$$

So:

$$2x=14$$

$$x=7$$

Step 3: Plug x = 7 into one original equation.

$$7+y=10$$

$$y=3$$

Answer:

$$x=7,\ y=3$$

Teacher note:
This practice matches the teaching example: one variable already cancels.

blocks.title: Elimination Level 2: Multiply First, Then Cancel
blocks.content:
Sometimes nothing cancels right away.

That does not mean elimination is impossible.

It means you first multiply one equation to make a variable match.

Tiny teaching example:

$$x+y=6$$

$$2x-y=3$$

This one already has +y and -y, so Level 1 works.

But now compare this:

$$x+y=6$$

$$2x+3y=16$$

Nothing cancels.

To use elimination, make one variable match.

Multiply the first equation by 3:

$$3(x+y=6)$$

$$3x+3y=18$$

Now compare:

$$3x+3y=18$$

$$2x+3y=16$$

The y terms match.

Subtract the equations:

$$(3x+3y)-(2x+3y)=18-16$$

$$x=2$$

Teacher note:
Elimination can include multiplying first. The goal is still the same: make one variable disappear.

blocks.title: Practice — Elimination With Multiply First
blocks.content:
Problem:
Solve the system.

$$2x+y=8$$

$$x+2y=7$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Nothing cancels right away.

So we make one variable match.

The first equation has y.

The second equation has 2y.

Multiply the first equation by 2:

$$2(2x+y=8)$$

$$4x+2y=16$$

Step 2: Compare with the second equation.

$$4x+2y=16$$

$$x+2y=7$$

Now both equations have +2y.

Step 3: Subtract to cancel y.

$$(4x+2y)-(x+2y)=16-7$$

$$3x=9$$

$$x=3$$

Step 4: Plug x = 3 into an original equation.

$$2(3)+y=8$$

$$6+y=8$$

$$y=2$$

Answer:

$$x=3,\ y=2$$

Teacher note:
This practice matches the previous lesson: multiply first to make a variable match, then subtract.

blocks.title: Substitution Level 1: One Variable Is Already Alone
blocks.content:
Use substitution when one equation already tells you what a variable equals.

Tiny teaching example:

$$y=2x+1$$

$$3x+y=16$$

The first equation already says:

$$y=2x+1$$

So replace y in the second equation with $2x+1$:

$$3x+(2x+1)=16$$

$$5x+1=16$$

$$5x=15$$

$$x=3$$

Now find y:

$$y=2(3)+1=7$$

Answer:

$$x=3,\ y=7$$

Teacher note:
Substitution means replacing a variable with what it equals.

blocks.title: Practice — Substitution When y Is Already Alone
blocks.content:
Problem:
Solve the system.

$$2x+3y=12$$

$$y=x-1$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Notice y is already alone.

$$y=x-1$$

Step 2: Replace y in the first equation with x - 1.

$$2x+3(x-1)=12$$

Step 3: Solve.

$$2x+3x-3=12$$

$$5x-3=12$$

$$5x=15$$

$$x=3$$

Step 4: Find y.

$$y=x-1$$

$$y=3-1=2$$

Answer:

$$x=3,\ y=2$$

Teacher note:
This practice matches the previous lesson: y was already alone, so substitute.

blocks.title: Substitution Level 2: Rewrite One Equation First
blocks.content:
Sometimes no variable is alone, but one equation is easy to rewrite.

Tiny teaching example:

$$x+y=6$$

$$2x+3y=14$$

From the first equation:

$$x+y=6$$

Solve for x:

$$x=6-y$$

Now substitute into the second equation:

$$2(6-y)+3y=14$$

$$12-2y+3y=14$$

$$12+y=14$$

$$y=2$$

Then:

$$x=6-2=4$$

Answer:

$$x=4,\ y=2$$

Teacher note:
If an equation has x + y = number, it is often easy to rewrite one variable.

blocks.title: Practice — Substitution After Rewriting
blocks.content:
Problem:
Solve the system.

$$3x+2y=13$$

$$x+y=5$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Use the easier equation.

$$x+y=5$$

Solve for x:

$$x=5-y$$

Step 2: Substitute into the other equation.

$$3(5-y)+2y=13$$

Step 3: Solve.

$$15-3y+2y=13$$

$$15-y=13$$

$$-y=-2$$

$$y=2$$

Step 4: Find x.

$$x=5-y$$

$$x=5-2=3$$

Answer:

$$x=3,\ y=2$$

Teacher note:
This practice matches the previous lesson: rewrite one simple equation first, then substitute.

blocks.title: Age Problems Level 1: Older Than + Past Condition
blocks.content:
Age problems are systems because they usually give two conditions.

Use this order:

1. Define current ages first.

2. Translate the current-age relationship.

3. Translate the past or future relationship.

4. Substitute.

Tiny teaching example:
Maria is 4 years older than Leo.
Four years ago, Maria was twice as old as Leo.
How old is Maria now?

Let:

$$M=\text{Maria's current age}$$

$$L=\text{Leo's current age}$$

Current relationship:

$$M=L+4$$

Four years ago, subtract 4 from both people:

Maria four years ago:

$$M-4$$

Leo four years ago:

$$L-4$$

“Maria was twice as old as Leo” becomes:

$$M-4=2(L-4)$$

Then substitute $M=L+4$:

$$(L+4)-4=2(L-4)$$

$$L=2L-8$$

$$L=8$$

Then:

$$M=L+4=12$$

Answer: Maria is 12.

Teacher note:
Past/future changes everyone, not just one person.

blocks.title: Practice — Age Problem With Older Than and Past
blocks.content:
Problem:
Abigail is 6 years older than Jonathan. Six years ago, Abigail was twice as old as Jonathan. How old is Abigail now?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Define current ages.

$$A=\text{Abigail's current age}$$

$$J=\text{Jonathan's current age}$$

Step 2: Translate “Abigail is 6 years older.”

$$A=J+6$$

Step 3: Translate “six years ago.”

Abigail six years ago:

$$A-6$$

Jonathan six years ago:

$$J-6$$

“Abigail was twice as old as Jonathan” becomes:

$$A-6=2(J-6)$$

Step 4: Substitute $A=J+6$.

$$(J+6)-6=2(J-6)$$

$$J=2J-12$$

$$J=12$$

Step 5: Find Abigail's age.

$$A=J+6$$

$$A=12+6=18$$

Answer: Abigail is 18.

Teacher note:
This practice matches the teaching example: older-than relationship plus a past condition.

blocks.title: Age Problems Level 2: Times As Old + Future Condition
blocks.content:
Some age problems compare ages using multiplication.

Tiny teaching example:
A father is currently 4 times as old as his son.
In 6 years, the father will be twice as old as his son.
How old is the son now?

Let:

$$F=\text{father's current age}$$

$$S=\text{son's current age}$$

Current condition:

$$F=4S$$

In 6 years, add 6 to both people.

Father in 6 years:

$$F+6$$

Son in 6 years:

$$S+6$$

“Father will be twice as old as son” becomes:

$$F+6=2(S+6)$$

Substitute $F=4S$:

$$4S+6=2(S+6)$$

$$4S+6=2S+12$$

$$2S=6$$

$$S=3$$

Answer: the son is 3 now.

Teacher note:
The sentence is about future ages, so create future ages first, then compare them.

blocks.title: Practice — Age Problem With Times As Old and Future
blocks.content:
Problem:
A mother is currently 3 times as old as her daughter. In 5 years, the mother will be twice as old as her daughter. How old is the daughter now?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Define current ages.

$$M=\text{mother's current age}$$

$$D=\text{daughter's current age}$$

Step 2: Translate the current condition.

Mother is currently 3 times daughter's age:

$$M=3D$$

Step 3: Translate the future condition.

In 5 years, add 5 to both people.

Mother in 5 years:

$$M+5$$

Daughter in 5 years:

$$D+5$$

Mother will be twice daughter:

$$M+5=2(D+5)$$

Step 4: Substitute $M=3D$.

$$3D+5=2(D+5)$$

$$3D+5=2D+10$$

$$D=5$$

Answer: the daughter is 5 now.

Teacher note:
This practice matches the teaching example: times-as-old now plus a future condition.

blocks.title: Word Translation: Exceeds By
blocks.content:
The phrase “A exceeds B by C” means:

A is C more than B.

The most intuitive equation is:

$$A=B+C$$

You may also see the same idea written as:

$$A-B=C$$

Both forms are correct.

Tiny teaching example:

“Three times x exceeds y by 8.”

A = three times x:

$$3x$$

B = y

C = 8

Use:

$$A=B+C$$

So:

$$3x=y+8$$

Equivalent subtraction form:

$$3x-y=8$$

Teacher note:
If “exceeds by” confuses you, read it as “is more than by.”

blocks.title: Practice — Exceeds By Translation
blocks.content:
Problem:
Translate into an equation.

Two times r exceeds one-half of t by 5.
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Identify A, B, and C.

A = two times r:

$$2r$$

B = one-half of t:

$$\frac{1}{2}t$$

C = 5

Step 2: Use the clearer pattern.

A exceeds B by C means:

$$A=B+C$$

So:

$$2r=\frac{1}{2}t+5$$

Step 3: Rewrite if the answer choices use subtraction form.

Subtract $\frac{1}{2}t$ from both sides:

$$2r-\frac{1}{2}t=5$$

Answer:

$$2r=\frac{1}{2}t+5$$

or:

$$2r-\frac{1}{2}t=5$$

Teacher note:
Both forms are the same equation. The test may list either one.

blocks.title: Money and Ticket Problems: Total Count + Total Value
blocks.content:
Money or ticket systems usually have two equations.

Equation 1 counts how many items there are.

Equation 2 counts the total value.

Tiny teaching example:
A theater sold 10 tickets.
Adult tickets cost $8.
Child tickets cost $5.
The total money collected was $68.
How many adult tickets were sold?

Let:

$$a=\text{adult tickets}$$

$$c=\text{child tickets}$$

Total tickets:

$$a+c=10$$

Total money:

$$8a+5c=68$$

Teacher note:
One equation counts items. The other equation counts dollars.

blocks.title: Practice — Ticket System
blocks.content:
Problem:
A school sold 12 tickets. Student tickets cost $4 and adult tickets cost $7. The total money collected was $66. How many adult tickets were sold?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Define variables.

$$s=\text{student tickets}$$

$$a=\text{adult tickets}$$

Step 2: Write total ticket equation.

$$s+a=12$$

Step 3: Write total money equation.

$$4s+7a=66$$

Step 4: Use substitution from the first equation.

$$s=12-a$$

Step 5: Substitute.

$$4(12-a)+7a=66$$

$$48-4a+7a=66$$

$$48+3a=66$$

$$3a=18$$

$$a=6$$

Answer: 6 adult tickets.

Teacher note:
This practice matches the teaching example: total count plus total money.

blocks.title: Investment Problems: Total Money + Total Interest
blocks.content:
Investment systems are like money problems.

They also usually have two equations.

Equation 1: total money.

Equation 2: total interest.

Tiny teaching example:
A person invests $200 total.
Some earns 4% interest.
Some earns 6% interest.
Total interest after one year is $10.
How much was invested at 6%?

Let:

$$x=\text{money at 4%}$$

$$y=\text{money at 6%}$$

Total money:

$$x+y=200$$

Total interest:

$$0.04x+0.06y=10$$

Teacher note:
Percent interest must be changed to decimal. 4% becomes 0.04.

blocks.title: Practice — Investment System
blocks.content:
Problem:
A sailor invests $100 in two accounts. One earns 5% and one earns 10%. After one year, the total interest is $8. How much was invested in the 10% account?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Define variables.

$$x=\text{amount in 5% account}$$

$$y=\text{amount in 10% account}$$

Step 2: Write total money equation.

$$x+y=100$$

Step 3: Write total interest equation.

$$0.05x+0.10y=8$$

Step 4: Use substitution.

From the first equation:

$$x=100-y$$

Step 5: Substitute.

$$0.05(100-y)+0.10y=8$$

$$5-0.05y+0.10y=8$$

$$5+0.05y=8$$

$$0.05y=3$$

$$y=60$$

Answer: $60 was invested in the 10% account.

Teacher note:
This practice matches the teaching example: total money plus total interest.

blocks.title: Mixture Problems: Total Value of Ingredients = Total Value of Blend
blocks.content:
Mixture problems compare total value.

Use this idea:

value of part 1 + value of part 2 = value of final mixture

For price mixtures:

$$\text{price}_1\times\text{amount}_1+\text{price}_2\times\text{amount}_2=\text{blend price}\times\text{total amount}$$

Tiny teaching example:
4 pounds of coffee costs $1.50 per pound.
x pounds of another coffee costs $2.50 per pound.
The final blend costs $2.00 per pound.
Find x.

Value of first coffee:

$$1.50(4)$$

Value of second coffee:

$$2.50x$$

Total pounds in final blend:

$$4+x$$

Value of final blend:

$$2.00(4+x)$$

Equation:

$$1.50(4)+2.50x=2.00(4+x)$$

Teacher note:
Mixture equations compare total value, not just pounds.

blocks.title: Practice — Mixture Problem
blocks.content:
Problem:
A grocer mixes 5 pounds of nuts costing $3 per pound with x pounds of nuts costing $6 per pound. The final mixture costs $4 per pound. Find x.
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Use the mixture idea.

value of part 1 + value of part 2 = value of final mixture

Step 2: Write the value of each part.

First nuts:

$$3(5)$$

Second nuts:

$$6x$$

Final mixture:

The total pounds are:

$$5+x$$

The blend costs $4 per pound, so final value is:

$$4(5+x)$$

Step 3: Set up the equation.

$$3(5)+6x=4(5+x)$$

Step 4: Solve.

$$15+6x=20+4x$$

$$2x=5$$

$$x=2.5$$

Answer: 2.5 pounds.

Teacher note:
This practice matches the teaching example: compare total value before and after mixing.

blocks.title: Three-Variable Systems: Same Idea, More Variables
blocks.content:
A three-variable system has x, y, and z.

This is elimination with one extra variable.

Tiny teaching example:

$$x+y+z=9$$

$$x-y+z=3$$

$$x+y-z=5$$

Add the second and third equations:

$$(x-y+z)+(x+y-z)=3+5$$

The y and z cancel:

$$2x=8$$

$$x=4$$

Then plug into simpler combinations to find the other variables.

Teacher note:
Three-variable systems are later-level. The basic idea is still cancel variables.

blocks.title: Practice — Three-Variable System
blocks.content:
Problem:
Solve for x.

$$x+y+z=12$$

$$x-y+z=6$$

$$x+y-z=8$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Add the second and third equations.

$$(x-y+z)+(x+y-z)=6+8$$

The y terms cancel.

The z terms cancel.

$$2x=14$$

$$x=7$$

Answer: x = 7.

Teacher note:
This practice matches the teaching example: add two equations so two variables cancel at once.

blocks.title: Nonlinear Systems: Substitute, Then Solve the Quadratic
blocks.content:
A nonlinear system has at least one equation with something like $x^2$ or $y^2$.

Use substitution.

Tiny teaching example:

$$x^2+y^2=25$$

$$x+y=7$$

Use the line equation to solve for y:

$$y=7-x$$

Substitute into the circle equation:

$$x^2+(7-x)^2=25$$

Then solve the quadratic.

Teacher note:
Nonlinear systems connect systems and quadratics. Treat them as later-level practice.

blocks.title: Practice — Nonlinear System Setup
blocks.content:
Problem:
Set up the equation after substitution.

$$x^2+y^2=20$$

$$x+y=6$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Solve the linear equation for y.

$$x+y=6$$

$$y=6-x$$

Step 2: Substitute into the nonlinear equation.

Original nonlinear equation:

$$x^2+y^2=20$$

Replace y with $6-x$:

$$x^2+(6-x)^2=20$$

Answer:

$$x^2+(6-x)^2=20$$

Teacher note:
This practice matches the teaching example: substitute the line into the equation with squares.

blocks.title: Special OAR Pattern: Consecutive Squares
blocks.content:
Some OAR-style questions use years written as squares.

Tiny teaching example:
A war starts in year $x^2$ and ends in year $(x+1)^2$.
It lasted 29 years.
Find the start year.

Duration means:

end year minus start year

So:

$$(x+1)^2-x^2=29$$

Expand:

$$x^2+2x+1-x^2=29$$

$$2x+1=29$$

$$2x=28$$

$$x=14$$

Start year:

$$x^2=14^2=196$$

Answer: 196.

Teacher note:
The shortcut is $(x+1)^2-x^2=2x+1$.

blocks.title: Practice — Consecutive Squares Pattern
blocks.content:
Problem:
An event starts in year $x^2$ and ends in year $(x+1)^2$. It lasts 35 years. What is the start year?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Duration means end minus start.

$$(x+1)^2-x^2=35$$

Step 2: Use the shortcut.

$$(x+1)^2-x^2=2x+1$$

So:

$$2x+1=35$$

Step 3: Solve.

$$2x=34$$

$$x=17$$

Step 4: Find the start year.

$$x^2=17^2=289$$

Answer: 289.

Teacher note:
This practice matches the teaching example: consecutive square years.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Systems
blocks.content:
Keep this at the end as a summary.

SYSTEM:

More than one equation must be true at the same time.

METHOD CHOICE:

Elimination:

Use when equations are lined up and you can cancel a variable.

If nothing cancels, multiply one equation first to make a match.

Substitution:

Use when one variable is already alone or easy to isolate.

AGE:

Current ages first.

In k years: add k to everyone.

k years ago: subtract k from everyone.

EXCEEDS BY:

A exceeds B by C means:

$$A=B+C$$

or:

$$A-B=C$$

MONEY / TICKETS:

Total count:

$$x+y=\text{total}$$

Total value:

$$p_1x+p_2y=\text{total value}$$

INVESTMENT:

Total money:

$$x+y=\text{total money}$$

Total interest:

$$r_1x+r_2y=\text{interest}$$

MIXTURE:

$$\text{price}_1\times\text{amount}_1+\text{price}_2\times\text{amount}_2=\text{blend price}\times\text{total amount}$$

CONSECUTIVE SQUARES:

$$(x+1)^2-x^2=2x+1$$

Teacher note:
Systems do not require a giant formula sheet. The main skill is translating clearly.

key_formulas: SYSTEM MEANING: more than one equation must be true at the same time.
ELIMINATION: cancel one variable. If needed, multiply one equation first to make a match.
SUBSTITUTION: replace a variable with what it equals.
AGE: current ages first; future add years to everyone; past subtract years from everyone.
common_mistakes: Teaching/practicing mismatch: do not practice a harder variation until the missing step is taught.
Changing only one person's age in an age problem.
Forgetting that elimination can require multiplying one equation first.
Reversing the phrase exceeds by.
Tracking amounts but forgetting total value or interest.
Letting broken LaTeX make the equation unreadable.

conceptPages.title: Mixture Problems
conceptPages.overview: Mixture problems are percent, cost, and systems problems disguised as acid, salt, coffee, tickets, alloys, or tanks. The test-solving goal is to track the important part: acid, salt, copper, antifreeze, or total money. This lesson teaches one pattern at a time, and each practice problem matches the strategy taught right before it.

blocks.title: Start Here: What Are We Tracking?
blocks.content:
A mixture problem has two things:

1. The total amount.

2. The important part inside the total.

Examples:

Acid solution: track acid.

Salt solution: track salt.

Alloy: track copper.

Antifreeze solution: track antifreeze.

Coffee or nuts by price: track total cost.

Tickets: track total money.

Teacher note:
Do not try to track the whole story. Track the important part.

blocks.title: The Core Idea: Important Part = Percent × Total Amount
blocks.content:
The most important mixture move is:

$$\text{important part}=\text{percent as decimal}\times\text{total amount}$$

Tiny teaching example:
A 40% acid solution has 12 ounces total.

The important part is acid.

$$0.40(12)=4.8$$

So the solution contains 4.8 ounces of acid.

Teacher note:
Percent must be changed to a decimal. 40% becomes 0.40.

blocks.title: Practice — Find the Important Part
blocks.content:
Problem:
A 30% salt solution has 20 liters total. How many liters of salt are in the solution?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Identify the important part.

The important part is salt.

Step 2: Convert the percent to a decimal.

30% = 0.30.

Step 3: Multiply percent by total amount.

$$0.30(20)=6$$

Answer: 6 liters of salt.

Teacher note:
This is the basic move behind almost every mixture problem.

blocks.title: Add Pure Substance: Pure Means 100%
blocks.content:
Use this when the problem adds pure acid, pure copper, pure antifreeze, or another pure substance.

Pure substance means 100%, so use 1.00.

Tiny teaching example:
You have 10 ounces of 30% acid. You add x ounces of pure acid to make a 50% solution.

Start acid:

$$0.30(10)$$

Added pure acid:

$$1.00x$$

Final total amount:

$$10+x$$

Final acid:

$$0.50(10+x)$$

Equation:

$$0.30(10)+1.00x=0.50(10+x)$$

Teacher note:
Left side = acid before plus acid added. Right side = acid in the final mixture.

blocks.title: Practice — Add Pure Substance
blocks.content:
Problem:
How much pure acid must be added to 12 ounces of a 40% acid solution to produce a 60% acid solution?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Let x = ounces of pure acid added.

Pure acid is 100%, so use 1.00x.

Step 2: Write acid before plus acid added equals acid after.

$$0.40(12)+1.00x=0.60(12+x)$$

Step 3: Simplify.

$$4.8+x=7.2+0.6x$$

Step 4: Solve.

$$x-0.6x=7.2-4.8$$

$$0.4x=2.4$$

$$x=6$$

Answer: 6 ounces.

Teacher note:
The final total is 12 + x because the amount you add increases the total volume.

blocks.title: Add Water: Water Means 0%
blocks.content:
Use this when water is added to dilute a solution.

Water has 0% acid or 0% salt.

That means water adds volume but no acid or salt.

Tiny teaching example:
You have 20 liters of 30% acid. You add x liters of water to make it 20% acid.

Start acid:

$$0.30(20)=6$$

Added acid from water:

$$0x=0$$

Final total volume:

$$20+x$$

Equation:

$$6=0.20(20+x)$$

Teacher note:
When adding water, the important part stays the same. Only the total amount changes.

blocks.title: Practice — Add Water
blocks.content:
Problem:
Water is added to a 20% salt solution to produce 30 liters of a 15% salt solution. How many liters of water are added?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Let x = original liters of 20% salt solution.

The final amount is 30 liters.

Step 2: Salt before equals salt after because water adds no salt.

$$0.20x=0.15(30)$$

Step 3: Solve for the original amount.

$$0.20x=4.5$$

$$x=22.5$$

So the original solution was 22.5 liters.

Step 4: Find water added.

$$30-22.5=7.5$$

Answer: 7.5 liters of water.

Teacher note:
This question gives the final amount, not the starting amount. That is why we first find the original amount.

blocks.title: Two Solutions Mixed to a Known Final Amount
blocks.content:
Use this when two solutions are mixed and the final total amount is given.

If the final total is 25 liters and one solution is x liters, the other solution is:

$$25-x$$

Tiny teaching example:
How many liters of 30% alcohol are mixed with 50% alcohol to make 25 liters of 38% alcohol?

Let x = liters of 30% solution.

Then 25 - x = liters of 50% solution.

Equation:

$$0.30x+0.50(25-x)=0.38(25)$$

Teacher note:
When the final total is fixed, use x and total minus x.

blocks.title: Practice — Two Solutions with Final Total
blocks.content:
Problem:
How many liters of a 30% alcohol solution must be mixed with a 50% alcohol solution to obtain 25 liters of a 38% solution?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Let x = liters of 30% solution.

Then 25 - x = liters of 50% solution.

Step 2: Write alcohol from each solution equals alcohol in final solution.

$$0.30x+0.50(25-x)=0.38(25)$$

Step 3: Simplify.

$$0.30x+12.5-0.50x=9.5$$

$$-0.20x=-3$$

$$x=15$$

Answer: 15 liters of the 30% solution.

Teacher note:
The other solution would be 25 - 15 = 10 liters.

blocks.title: Price Mixtures: Track Total Cost
blocks.content:
Price mixture problems use the same idea, but the important part is money.

Instead of percent times amount, use price times quantity.

Tiny teaching example:
A blend uses x pounds of $4 coffee and 30 - x pounds of $7 coffee to make 30 pounds of $5 coffee.

Cost of $4 coffee:

$$4x$$

Cost of $7 coffee:

$$7(30-x)$$

Cost of final blend:

$$5(30)$$

Equation:

$$4x+7(30-x)=5(30)$$

Teacher note:
For price mixtures, do not track acid or salt. Track total cost.

blocks.title: Practice — Price Mixture
blocks.content:
Problem:
Coffee beans at $4 per pound are mixed with coffee beans at $7 per pound to produce 30 pounds of a blend selling for $5 per pound. How many pounds of the $4 beans are used?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Let x = pounds of $4 beans.

Then 30 - x = pounds of $7 beans.

Step 2: Write total cost equation.

$$4x+7(30-x)=5(30)$$

Step 3: Simplify.

$$4x+210-7x=150$$

$$-3x=-60$$

$$x=20$$

Answer: 20 pounds of the $4 beans.

Teacher note:
The final blend is 30 pounds at $5 per pound, so the final total cost is 5(30).

blocks.title: Ticket Problems: Count Equation + Money Equation
blocks.content:
Ticket problems are like price mixtures, but they usually need two equations.

One equation counts the tickets.

One equation counts the money.

Tiny teaching example:
Adult tickets cost $8. Child tickets cost $5. A total of 200 tickets are sold for $1,300.

Let a = adult tickets.

Let c = child tickets.

Count equation:

$$a+c=200$$

Money equation:

$$8a+5c=1300$$

Teacher note:
The first equation tracks how many. The second equation tracks dollars.

blocks.title: Practice — Ticket Price System
blocks.content:
Problem:
A theater sells adult tickets at $8 and child tickets at $5. 200 tickets were sold for a total of $1,300. How many adult tickets were sold?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Define variables.

a = adult tickets.

c = child tickets.

Step 2: Write count equation.

$$a+c=200$$

Step 3: Write money equation.

$$8a+5c=1300$$

Step 4: Substitute c = 200 - a.

$$8a+5(200-a)=1300$$

Step 5: Solve.

$$8a+1000-5a=1300$$

$$3a=300$$

$$a=100$$

Answer: 100 adult tickets.

Teacher note:
This is a system because there are two unknowns and two conditions.

blocks.title: Ratio Mixtures: Use the Parts as Amounts
blocks.content:
Use this when the problem gives a ratio, like 3 parts to 2 parts.

You can treat the parts like amounts.

Tiny teaching example:
A 20% solution and a 50% solution are mixed in a ratio of 3 parts to 2 parts.

Use 3 units of the 20% solution.

Use 2 units of the 50% solution.

Total important part:

$$0.20(3)+0.50(2)$$

Total amount:

$$3+2=5$$

Final concentration:

$$\frac{0.20(3)+0.50(2)}{5}$$

Teacher note:
A ratio mixture is a weighted average.

blocks.title: Practice — Ratio Mixture
blocks.content:
Problem:
Two solutions are mixed in a ratio of 3 parts to 2 parts. The first solution is 20% acid and the second is 50% acid. What is the acid concentration of the final mixture?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Use the ratio as amounts.

Use 3 parts of the 20% solution.

Use 2 parts of the 50% solution.

Step 2: Find acid from each part.

$$0.20(3)=0.6$$

$$0.50(2)=1.0$$

Step 3: Add acid and total parts.

Total acid:

$$0.6+1.0=1.6$$

Total amount:

$$3+2=5$$

Step 4: Divide.

$$\frac{1.6}{5}=0.32$$

Answer: 32%.

Teacher note:
The final concentration should be between 20% and 50%.

blocks.title: Drain and Replace: Do It in Two Steps
blocks.content:
Drain-and-replace problems should be done in two steps.

Step 1: Drain some solution.

When you drain solution, you remove the same concentration as the original mixture.

Step 2: Add the replacement liquid.

Pure water adds 0% acid or salt.

Pure antifreeze adds 100% antifreeze.

Tiny teaching example:
A tank has 20 liters of 40% antifreeze. 5 liters are drained and replaced with pure antifreeze.

Start antifreeze:

$$0.40(20)=8$$

Antifreeze removed:

$$0.40(5)=2$$

Antifreeze left:

$$8-2=6$$

Pure antifreeze added:

$$5$$

New antifreeze amount:

$$6+5=11$$

New total volume is still 20 liters.

New concentration:

$$\frac{11}{20}=55\%$$

Teacher note:
Do not average 40% and 100%. Track the actual amount of important liquid.

blocks.title: Practice — Drain and Replace with Water
blocks.content:
Problem:
A tank contains 50 liters of a 60% acid solution. 10 liters are drained and replaced with pure water. What is the new acid concentration?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Find starting acid.

$$0.60(50)=30$$

Step 2: Find acid removed.

The drained liquid is still 60% acid.

$$0.60(10)=6$$

Step 3: Find acid left.

$$30-6=24$$

Step 4: Add water.

Water adds 0 acid, so acid stays 24 liters.

The tank returns to 50 liters total.

Step 5: Find new concentration.

$$\frac{24}{50}=0.48$$

Answer: 48%.

Teacher note:
After replacing with water, the total volume is back to 50 liters, but the acid amount is lower.

blocks.title: Evaporation: Important Part Stays, Water Leaves
blocks.content:
Evaporation removes water, not salt or acid.

That means the important part stays the same, but the total amount gets smaller.

Tiny teaching example:
A tank has 40 liters of 10% salt solution. Water evaporates until the solution is 20% salt.

Start salt:

$$0.10(40)=4$$

Let V = new total volume.

Salt after evaporation:

$$0.20V$$

Set salt before equal to salt after:

$$4=0.20V$$

Teacher note:
Evaporation is not the same as draining solution. Evaporation removes water only.

blocks.title: Practice — Evaporation
blocks.content:
Problem:
A tank holds 40 liters of a 10% salt solution. How many liters of water must be evaporated to increase the concentration to 20%?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Find starting salt.

$$0.10(40)=4$$

Step 2: Let V = final volume after evaporation.

Salt stays 4 liters.

Final solution is 20% salt:

$$4=0.20V$$

Step 3: Solve for final volume.

$$V=20$$

Step 4: Find water evaporated.

$$40-20=20$$

Answer: 20 liters.

Teacher note:
The final volume is not the answer. The question asks how much water evaporated.

blocks.title: Three-Solution Mixtures: Use the Extra Clue
blocks.content:
A three-solution mixture usually gives an extra clue, such as one amount is twice another amount.

Do not try to guess the three amounts.

Write three relationships:

1. Total amount equation.

2. Important part equation.

3. Extra clue equation.

Tiny teaching example:
A chemist uses 10%, 20%, and 30% acid solutions to make 20 liters of a 22% solution. She uses twice as much 30% solution as 10% solution.

Let x = liters of 10%.

Let y = liters of 20%.

Let z = liters of 30%.

Total amount:

$$x+y+z=20$$

Acid amount:

$$0.10x+0.20y+0.30z=0.22(20)$$

Extra clue:

$$z=2x$$

Teacher note:
The extra clue is what makes the three unknowns possible to solve.

blocks.title: Practice — Three-Solution Mixture
blocks.content:
Problem:
Three solutions are available: 10%, 20%, and 30% acid. A chemist needs to make 20 liters of a 22% solution. She uses twice as much of the 30% solution as the 10% solution. How many liters of the 20% solution does she use?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Define variables.

x = liters of 10% solution.

y = liters of 20% solution.

z = liters of 30% solution.

Step 2: Write the extra clue.

$$z=2x$$

Step 3: Write total amount equation.

$$x+y+z=20$$

Substitute z = 2x:

$$x+y+2x=20$$

$$3x+y=20$$

$$y=20-3x$$

Step 4: Write acid equation.

$$0.10x+0.20y+0.30z=0.22(20)$$

Substitute y = 20 - 3x and z = 2x:

$$0.10x+0.20(20-3x)+0.30(2x)=4.4$$

Step 5: Simplify.

$$0.10x+4-0.60x+0.60x=4.4$$

$$0.10x+4=4.4$$

$$0.10x=0.4$$

$$x=4$$

Step 6: Find y.

$$y=20-3(4)=8$$

Answer: 8 liters of the 20% solution.

Teacher note:
This is a harder pattern. The setup matters more than speed.

blocks.title: Common Traps
blocks.content:
Trap 1: Forgetting to convert percent to decimal.

40% becomes 0.40.

Trap 2: Forgetting pure substance is 100%.

Use 1.00.

Trap 3: Forgetting water is 0%.

Use 0.

Trap 4: Using the starting total when the final total is different.

If you add x ounces, final total is original + x.

Trap 5: In drain-and-replace, forgetting that drained liquid removes some of the important part.

Trap 6: In evaporation, treating it like draining solution.

Evaporation removes water only.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Mixture Problems
blocks.content:
Keep this at the end as a summary.

CORE IDEA:

$$\text{important part}=\text{percent}\times\text{total amount}$$

BASIC MIXTURE:

$$P_1A_1+P_2A_2=P_fA_f$$

P = percent as decimal.

A = amount.

Pure substance:

$$100\%=1.00$$

Water:

$$0\%=0$$

FINAL TOTAL:

If adding x:

$$A_f=A_{start}+x$$

If final amount is fixed:

$$x \text{ and } A_f-x$$

PRICE MIXTURE:

$$\text{price}_1\times\text{amount}_1+\text{price}_2\times\text{amount}_2=\text{final price}\times\text{final amount}$$

TICKET SYSTEM:

$$a+c=\text{total tickets}$$

$$\text{adult price}a+\text{child price}c=\text{total money}$$

DRAIN AND REPLACE:

1. Find important part before.

2. Subtract important part removed.

3. Add important part from replacement.

4. Divide by final total.

EVAPORATION:

Important part stays the same.

Teacher note:
The most important question is always: what important part am I tracking?

key_formulas: CORE — important part: $$\text{important part}=\text{percent as decimal}\times\text{total amount}$$
FINAL TOTAL GIVEN — amounts: $$x \text{ and } A_f-x$$
TICKET SYSTEM: $$a+c=\text{total tickets};\quad p_a a+p_c c=\text{total money}$$
DRAIN AND REPLACE: important part left = important part before - important part removed + important part added
EVAPORATION: important part before = important part after
common_mistakes: Using 40 instead of 0.40 for 40%.
Forgetting pure substance is 1.00.
Forgetting water is 0.
Forgetting the final total changes when x is added.
Using x and total-x when the final total is not given.
Averaging concentrations without considering amounts.
In drain-and-replace problems, forgetting the drained liquid removes some of the important part.
In evaporation problems, treating evaporation like draining mixed solution instead of removing only water.
For price mixtures, tracking pounds but forgetting to track dollars.

conceptPages.title: Matrices
conceptPages.overview: Matrices are tables of numbers. Since this may be your first time learning them, this lesson starts with what rows, columns, positions, and dimensions mean before moving into matrix operations. The OAR-style goal is to recognize the operation, check the size rule, and do the arithmetic carefully.

blocks.title: Start Here: A Matrix Is Just a Table
blocks.content:
A matrix is a rectangular table of numbers.

Example:

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2&3\\4&5&6\end{pmatrix}$$

Rows go across.

Columns go down.

This matrix has 2 rows and 3 columns, so its size is:

$$2\times3$$

Teacher note:
Read matrix size as rows × columns. Rows first, columns second.

blocks.title: Matrix Position: Row Number, Column Number
blocks.content:
A matrix entry can be named by its position.

Position $(1,2)$ means:

row 1, column 2.

Example:

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2&3\\4&5&6\end{pmatrix}$$

Row 1 is:

$$1,2,3$$

Column 2 is:

$$2,5$$

The entry at position $(1,2)$ is 2.

The entry at position $(2,3)$ is 6.

Teacher note:
The order is row first, column second.

blocks.title: Practice — Matrix Size and Position
blocks.content:
Problem:
For the matrix

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}7&8&9\\10&11&12\end{pmatrix}$$

What is the size of the matrix, and what number is in position $(2,1)$?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Count rows.

There are 2 rows.

Step 2: Count columns.

There are 3 columns.

So the size is:

$$2\times3$$

Step 3: Find position $(2,1)$.

That means row 2, column 1.

Row 2 is:

$$10,11,12$$

The first number in row 2 is 10.

Answer: size is $2\times3$, and position $(2,1)$ is 10.

Teacher note:
Always read position as row first, column second.

blocks.title: Adding Matrices: Match Each Spot
blocks.content:
Matrix addition means add numbers in matching positions.

Important rule:

The matrices must have the same size.

Tiny teaching example:

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\3&4\end{pmatrix},\quad B=\begin{pmatrix}5&6\\7&8\end{pmatrix}$$

Add matching spots:

$$A+B=\begin{pmatrix}1+5&2+6\\3+7&4+8\end{pmatrix}$$

$$A+B=\begin{pmatrix}6&8\\10&12\end{pmatrix}$$

Teacher note:
Do not multiply. Just add the same positions.

blocks.title: Practice — Matrix Addition
blocks.content:
Problem:
Add the matrices.

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}2&3\\4&5\end{pmatrix},\quad B=\begin{pmatrix}6&7\\8&9\end{pmatrix}$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Check the sizes.

Both are $2\times2$, so addition is allowed.

Step 2: Add matching positions.

$$A+B=\begin{pmatrix}2+6&3+7\\4+8&5+9\end{pmatrix}$$

Step 3: Simplify.

$$A+B=\begin{pmatrix}8&10\\12&14\end{pmatrix}$$

Answer:

$$\begin{pmatrix}8&10\\12&14\end{pmatrix}$$

Teacher note:
Top-left adds to top-left. Top-right adds to top-right. Keep the positions lined up.

blocks.title: Scalar Multiplication: Multiply Every Entry
blocks.content:
A scalar is a regular number outside the matrix.

Scalar multiplication means multiply every entry by that number.

Tiny teaching example:

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}2&4\\6&8\end{pmatrix}$$

Find $3A$.

Multiply every entry by 3:

$$3A=\begin{pmatrix}3(2)&3(4)\\3(6)&3(8)\end{pmatrix}$$

$$3A=\begin{pmatrix}6&12\\18&24\end{pmatrix}$$

Teacher note:
Every entry gets multiplied. Do not only multiply the first row or first column.

blocks.title: Practice — Scalar Multiplication
blocks.content:
Problem:
Find $2A$.

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}3&5\\7&9\end{pmatrix}$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Multiply every entry by 2.

$$2A=\begin{pmatrix}2(3)&2(5)\\2(7)&2(9)\end{pmatrix}$$

Step 2: Simplify.

$$2A=\begin{pmatrix}6&10\\14&18\end{pmatrix}$$

Answer:

$$\begin{pmatrix}6&10\\14&18\end{pmatrix}$$

Teacher note:
Scalar multiplication is one of the easiest matrix operations if you remember every entry gets changed.

blocks.title: Transpose: Flip Rows and Columns
blocks.content:
Transpose means flip rows and columns.

Symbol:

$$A^T$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2&3\\4&5&6\end{pmatrix}$$

The first row becomes the first column.

The second row becomes the second column.

$$A^T=\begin{pmatrix}1&4\\2&5\\3&6\end{pmatrix}$$

The original matrix was $2\times3$.

The transpose is $3\times2$.

Teacher note:
Transpose switches the dimensions.

blocks.title: Practice — Transpose
blocks.content:
Problem:
Find the dimensions of $A^T$.

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2&3\\4&5&6\end{pmatrix}$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Find the size of A.

A has 2 rows and 3 columns.

So A is $2\times3$.

Step 2: Transpose switches rows and columns.

So $A^T$ is $3\times2$.

Answer: $3\times2$.

Teacher note:
You do not always need to write the full transpose if the question only asks for dimensions.

blocks.title: Matrix Multiplication Size Rule
blocks.content:
Matrix multiplication has a size rule.

For:

$$(a\times b)(c\times d)$$

multiplication is allowed only if the inside numbers match:

$$b=c$$

If it works, the answer size is the outside numbers:

$$a\times d$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$(2\times3)(3\times4)$$

The inside numbers are 3 and 3, so multiplication is allowed.

The answer size is:

$$2\times4$$

Teacher note:
Inside numbers decide whether multiplication is allowed. Outside numbers give the answer size.

blocks.title: Practice — Matrix Multiplication Dimensions
blocks.content:
Problem:
Matrix A is $2\times3$ and matrix B is $3\times4$. What are the dimensions of $AB$?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Write the sizes next to each other.

$$(2\times3)(3\times4)$$

Step 2: Check the inside numbers.

The inside numbers are 3 and 3.

They match, so $AB$ is defined.

Step 3: Use the outside numbers for the answer size.

Outside numbers are 2 and 4.

Answer: $2\times4$.

Teacher note:
You do not multiply all four numbers. Just check inside, then keep outside.

blocks.title: Undefined Multiplication: When Sizes Do Not Match
blocks.content:
If the inside dimensions do not match, matrix multiplication is undefined.

Tiny teaching example:

$$(2\times3)(4\times2)$$

The inside numbers are 3 and 4.

They do not match.

So this multiplication is not allowed.

Answer: undefined.

Teacher note:
Undefined does not mean the answer is 0. It means the multiplication cannot be done.

blocks.title: Practice — Undefined Matrix Multiplication
blocks.content:
Problem:
Which product is undefined?

A) $(3\times2)(2\times4)$

B) $(2\times5)(5\times1)$

C) $(4\times3)(2\times4)$

D) $(1\times4)(4\times2)$
blocks.solution:
Check the inside numbers for each option.

A) $(3\times2)(2\times4)$

Inside numbers: 2 and 2. Defined.

B) $(2\times5)(5\times1)$

Inside numbers: 5 and 5. Defined.

C) $(4\times3)(2\times4)$

Inside numbers: 3 and 2. Not defined.

D) $(1\times4)(4\times2)$

Inside numbers: 4 and 4. Defined.

Answer: C.

Teacher note:
For matrix multiplication, always check inside numbers first.

blocks.title: Matrix Multiplication Entry: Row Times Column
blocks.content:
To find one entry in $AB$, use:

row from A times column from B.

Tiny teaching example:

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\3&4\end{pmatrix},\quad B=\begin{pmatrix}5&6\\7&8\end{pmatrix}$$

Find the top-left entry of $AB$.

Top-left means position $(1,1)$.

Use row 1 of A and column 1 of B.

Row 1 of A:

$$1,2$$

Column 1 of B:

$$5,7$$

Multiply matching pieces and add:

$$1(5)+2(7)=5+14=19$$

Teacher note:
Matrix multiplication is multiply-and-add, not position-by-position multiplication.

blocks.title: Practice — Find One Entry of AB
blocks.content:
Problem:
Find the entry at position $(1,1)$ of $AB$.

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\4&3\end{pmatrix},\quad B=\begin{pmatrix}5&6\\7&8\end{pmatrix}$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Position $(1,1)$ means row 1 of A and column 1 of B.

Row 1 of A:

$$2,1$$

Column 1 of B:

$$5,7$$

Step 2: Multiply matching parts and add.

$$2(5)+1(7)=10+7=17$$

Answer: 17.

Teacher note:
Use row from the first matrix and column from the second matrix.

blocks.title: Full Matrix Product: Repeat Row Times Column
blocks.content:
To multiply full matrices, find each entry one at a time.

For a $2\times2$ answer, you find four entries:

$(1,1)$: row 1 of A with column 1 of B.

$(1,2)$: row 1 of A with column 2 of B.

$(2,1)$: row 2 of A with column 1 of B.

$(2,2)$: row 2 of A with column 2 of B.

Teacher note:
Do not try to do the whole matrix at once. Fill one position at a time.

blocks.title: Practice — Full Matrix Product
blocks.content:
Problem:
Compute $AB$.

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\3&4\end{pmatrix},\quad B=\begin{pmatrix}5&6\\7&8\end{pmatrix}$$
blocks.solution:
Find each entry.

Position $(1,1)$:

$$1(5)+2(7)=5+14=19$$

Position $(1,2)$:

$$1(6)+2(8)=6+16=22$$

Position $(2,1)$:

$$3(5)+4(7)=15+28=43$$

Position $(2,2)$:

$$3(6)+4(8)=18+32=50$$

So:

$$AB=\begin{pmatrix}19&22\\43&50\end{pmatrix}$$

Answer:

$$\begin{pmatrix}19&22\\43&50\end{pmatrix}$$

Teacher note:
Each entry is a separate row-column dot product.

blocks.title: Important Trap: AB Is Not Usually BA
blocks.content:
With regular numbers:

$$2\times3=3\times2$$

But matrix multiplication does not usually work that way.

Usually:

$$AB\ne BA$$

Sometimes one product is defined and the other is not.

Sometimes both are defined but have different sizes.

Sometimes both are defined and same size, but the numbers are different.

Teacher note:
Always check $AB$ and $BA$ separately.

blocks.title: Practice — AB and BA Dimensions
blocks.content:
Problem:
Matrix A is $3\times2$ and matrix B is $2\times3$.

Which statements are true?

I. $AB$ is defined and has size $3\times3$.

II. $BA$ is defined and has size $2\times2$.

III. $AB$ and $BA$ must be equal.
blocks.solution:
Statement I:

$$AB=(3\times2)(2\times3)$$

Inside numbers match, so it is defined.

Outside numbers give $3\times3$.

Statement I is true.

Statement II:

$$BA=(2\times3)(3\times2)$$

Inside numbers match, so it is defined.

Outside numbers give $2\times2$.

Statement II is true.

Statement III:

$AB$ is $3\times3$ and $BA$ is $2\times2$.

They cannot be equal because they are different sizes.

Statement III is false.

Answer: I and II only.

Teacher note:
Do not assume $AB=BA$.

blocks.title: Identity Matrix: The Matrix Version of 1
blocks.content:
The identity matrix acts like the number 1 in multiplication.

For a $2\times2$ matrix:

$$I=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}$$

Multiplying by the identity keeps the matrix the same:

$$AI=A$$

$$IA=A$$

Teacher note:
The identity matrix has 1s on the diagonal and 0s everywhere else.

blocks.title: Practice — Identity Matrix
blocks.content:
Problem:
Which statement is true about the identity matrix $I$?

A) $AI=A$ for compatible matrices.

B) $AI=0$ for compatible matrices.

C) $AI$ is always undefined.

D) $AI$ changes every entry in A.
blocks.solution:
The identity matrix acts like multiplying by 1.

So multiplying a compatible matrix by I keeps it the same.

$$AI=A$$

Answer: A.

Teacher note:
Identity matrix means no-change matrix for multiplication.

blocks.title: 2x2 Determinant
blocks.content:
For a $2\times2$ matrix:

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}$$

The determinant is:

$$ad-bc$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}3&2\\1&4\end{pmatrix}$$

$$\det(A)=3(4)-2(1)=12-2=10$$

Teacher note:
Think diagonal down minus diagonal up.

blocks.title: Practice — 2x2 Determinant
blocks.content:
Problem:
Find the determinant.

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}5&1\\2&3\end{pmatrix}$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Use the 2x2 determinant formula.

For

$$\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}$$

$$\det(A)=ad-bc$$

Step 2: Substitute.

$$a=5,\ b=1,\ c=2,\ d=3$$

$$\det(A)=5(3)-1(2)$$

$$\det(A)=15-2=13$$

Answer: 13.

Teacher note:
Multiply the main diagonal, then subtract the other diagonal.

blocks.title: 2x2 Inverse: Later-Level Pattern
blocks.content:
An inverse matrix is like the reciprocal version of a matrix.

For OAR-style practice, you may only need the $2\times2$ formula.

For:

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}$$

First find the determinant:

$$ad-bc$$

Then:

$$A^{-1}=\frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{pmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{pmatrix}$$

Tiny teaching example:

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}4&2\\3&1\end{pmatrix}$$

Determinant:

$$4(1)-2(3)=4-6=-2$$

Switch $a$ and $d$, and change signs of $b$ and $c$:

$$\begin{pmatrix}1&-2\\-3&4\end{pmatrix}$$

So:

$$A^{-1}=\frac{1}{-2}\begin{pmatrix}1&-2\\-3&4\end{pmatrix}$$

Teacher note:
If determinant is 0, the inverse does not exist.

blocks.title: Practice — 2x2 Inverse
blocks.content:
Problem:
Find the inverse setup for

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\5&3\end{pmatrix}$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Find the determinant.

$$ad-bc=2(3)-1(5)=6-5=1$$

Step 2: Switch $a$ and $d$.

2 and 3 switch places.

Step 3: Change signs of $b$ and $c$.

1 becomes -1.

5 becomes -5.

So:

$$A^{-1}=\frac{1}{1}\begin{pmatrix}3&-1\\-5&2\end{pmatrix}$$

Answer:

$$\begin{pmatrix}3&-1\\-5&2\end{pmatrix}$$

Teacher note:
The inverse formula is more memorization-heavy than addition or multiplication, so treat it as later-level review.

blocks.title: 3x3 Determinant: Cofactor Expansion
blocks.content:
A $3\times3$ determinant is harder.

The question bank uses cofactor expansion along the first row.

For:

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}a&b&c\\d&e&f\\g&h&i\end{pmatrix}$$

Expand along the first row:

$$\det(A)=a\begin{vmatrix}e&f\\h&i\end{vmatrix}-b\begin{vmatrix}d&f\\g&i\end{vmatrix}+c\begin{vmatrix}d&e\\g&h\end{vmatrix}$$

The signs are:

plus, minus, plus.

Teacher note:
This is a later-level pattern. If it appears, carefully compute the smaller 2x2 determinants.

blocks.title: Practice — 3x3 Determinant
blocks.content:
Problem:
Find the determinant.

$$A=\begin{pmatrix}2&1&0\\3&2&1\\1&0&2\end{pmatrix}$$
blocks.solution:
Expand along the first row:

$$2,1,0$$

Use plus, minus, plus.

Step 1: First term.

Remove row 1 and column 1:

$$\begin{vmatrix}2&1\\0&2\end{vmatrix}=2(2)-1(0)=4$$

First term:

$$2(4)=8$$

Step 2: Second term.

Remove row 1 and column 2:

$$\begin{vmatrix}3&1\\1&2\end{vmatrix}=3(2)-1(1)=5$$

Because the sign is minus:

$$-1(5)=-5$$

Step 3: Third term.

The third top-row entry is 0, so its whole term is 0.

Step 4: Add.

$$8-5+0=3$$

Answer: 3.

Teacher note:
A zero in the top row makes that part easier because zero times anything is zero.

blocks.title: Determinant of kA
blocks.content:
For an $n\times n$ matrix:

$$\det(kA)=k^n\det(A)$$

Tiny teaching example:
If A is $3\times3$ and $\det(A)=3$, then:

$$\det(2A)=2^3\det(A)$$

$$\det(2A)=8(3)=24$$

Teacher note:
The exponent is the size of the square matrix. For $3\times3$, use $k^3$.

blocks.title: Practice — Determinant of kA
blocks.content:
Problem:
Matrix A is $2\times2$ and $\det(A)=5$. What is $\det(3A)$?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Use the property.

For an $n\times n$ matrix:

$$\det(kA)=k^n\det(A)$$

Step 2: Identify values.

A is $2\times2$, so $n=2$.

$k=3$.

$$\det(A)=5$$

Step 3: Substitute.

$$\det(3A)=3^2(5)$$

$$\det(3A)=9(5)=45$$

Answer: 45.

Teacher note:
Do not just do $3\times5$. Because A is $2\times2$, multiply by $3^2$.

blocks.title: Cramer's Rule: Solving a System With Determinants
blocks.content:
Cramer's Rule solves a system using determinants.

For:

$$a x+b y=e$$

$$c x+d y=f$$
