Main determinant:

$$D=\begin{vmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{vmatrix}$$

To find x, replace the x-column with the answer column:

$$D_x=\begin{vmatrix}e&b\\f&d\end{vmatrix}$$

Then:

$$x=\frac{D_x}{D}$$

Teacher note:
Only use this when the problem specifically asks for Cramer's Rule or gives determinant-style answer choices.

blocks.title: Practice — Cramer's Rule
blocks.content:
Problem:
Use Cramer's Rule to solve for x.

$$2x+y=5$$

$$x+3y=10$$
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Build the main determinant from the coefficients.

$$D=\begin{vmatrix}2&1\\1&3\end{vmatrix}$$

$$D=2(3)-1(1)=6-1=5$$

Step 2: Build $D_x$ by replacing the x-column with the answer numbers 5 and 10.

$$D_x=\begin{vmatrix}5&1\\10&3\end{vmatrix}$$

$$D_x=5(3)-1(10)=15-10=5$$

Step 3: Divide.

$$x=\frac{D_x}{D}=\frac{5}{5}=1$$

Answer: x = 1.

Teacher note:
Cramer's Rule is a systems method. It is not the fastest way unless the question specifically asks for it.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Matrices
blocks.content:
Keep this at the very end as your matrix cheat sheet.

MATRIX BASICS

Size:

$$\text{rows}\times\text{columns}$$

Position:

$$(i,j)=\text{row }i,\text{ column }j$$

ADDITION

Same size required.

Add matching positions.

SCALAR MULTIPLICATION

Multiply every entry by the outside number.

TRANSPOSE

Flip rows and columns.

If A is $m\times n$, then $A^T$ is $n\times m$.

MATRIX MULTIPLICATION SIZE

$$(a\times b)(c\times d)$$

Defined only if:

$$b=c$$

Answer size:

$$a\times d$$

MATRIX MULTIPLICATION ENTRY

Entry $(i,j)$ of $AB$ = row $i$ of A times column $j$ of B.

2x2 DETERMINANT

$$\det\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}=ad-bc$$

2x2 INVERSE

$$A^{-1}=\frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{pmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{pmatrix}$$

IDENTITY MATRIX

$$AI=IA=A$$

IMPORTANT TRAP

Usually:

$$AB\ne BA$$

Teacher note:
For a first pass, prioritize size rule, addition, scalar multiplication, transpose, row-times-column multiplication, and 2x2 determinant.

key_formulas: POSITION: $(i,j)$ means row i, column j
ADDITION: same size required; add matching positions
SCALAR MULTIPLICATION: multiply every entry by the scalar
TRANSPOSE: if $A$ is $m\times n$, then $A^T$ is $n\times m$
MULTIPLICATION SIZE RULE: $$(a\times b)(c\times d)\text{ is defined only if }b=c$$
MULTIPLICATION ANSWER SIZE: $$(a\times b)(b\times c)=a\times c$$
MATRIX PRODUCT ENTRY: entry $(i,j)$ = row i of A dot column j of B
3x3 DETERMINANT: expand first row with signs plus, minus, plus
DETERMINANT SCALAR PROPERTY: $$\det(kA)=k^n\det(A)$$ for an $n\times n$ matrix
common_mistakes: Reading dimensions backward. Matrix size is rows × columns.
Adding matrices of different sizes.
For scalar multiplication, multiplying only one row or one column instead of every entry.
Thinking matrix multiplication is position-by-position multiplication.
For matrix multiplication, forgetting to check the inside dimensions first.
Assuming AB equals BA.
For determinants, doing ad + bc instead of ad - bc.
For inverse, forgetting to divide by the determinant.
For det(kA), multiplying by k instead of k^n.

conceptPages.title: Geometry
conceptPages.overview: Geometry questions are mostly formula-selection and setup problems. The goal is not to prove geometry. The goal is to recognize the shape, identify what the question asks for, pick the matching formula, and avoid common traps like using diameter as radius or using area when the question asks perimeter.

blocks.title: Start Here: Geometry Is Shape + Question Type
blocks.content:
For geometry, do this before touching a formula.

1. Draw the shape.

2. Label the numbers given.

3. Circle what the question asks for.

Ask:

Is it asking for length, perimeter, area, volume, or an angle?

Teacher note:
Most geometry mistakes happen because the formula is fine, but the wrong measurement is used. If the problem gives diameter and the formula uses radius, divide by 2 first.

blocks.title: Rectangle: Area vs. Perimeter
blocks.content:
Rectangle area means the space inside.

Formula:

$$A=lw$$

Rectangle perimeter means the distance around the outside.

Formula:

$$P=2l+2w$$

Tiny teaching example:
A rectangle has length 12 and width 5.

Area:

$$12\times5=60$$

Perimeter:

$$2(12)+2(5)=34$$

Teacher note:
Area uses square units. Perimeter uses regular length units.

blocks.title: Practice — Rectangle Perimeter
blocks.content:
Problem:
A rectangular supply room has a length of 12 feet and a width of 5 feet. What is the perimeter?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Perimeter means distance around the outside.

Use:

$$P=2l+2w$$

Step 2: Substitute.

$$P=2(12)+2(5)$$

$$P=24+10=34$$

Answer: 34 feet.

Teacher note:
Do not use 12 × 5 here. That would give area, not perimeter.

blocks.title: Triangle Area
blocks.content:
Triangle area uses base and height.

Formula:

$$A=\frac{1}{2}bh$$

Tiny teaching example:
A triangle has base 8 and height 5.

$$A=\frac{1}{2}(8)(5)=20$$

Why the 1/2?

A triangle is half of a rectangle with the same base and height.

Teacher note:
Base and height must meet at a right angle. The height is not always the slanted side.

blocks.title: Practice — Solve for Triangle Height
blocks.content:
Problem:
A triangle has an area of 36 cm² and a base of 9 cm. What is its height?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Start with triangle area.

$$A=\frac{1}{2}bh$$

Step 2: Substitute what you know.

$$36=\frac{1}{2}(9)h$$

$$36=4.5h$$

Step 3: Divide.

$$h=36\div4.5=8$$

Answer: 8 cm.

Teacher note:
You can also remember the rearranged shortcut: $$h=\frac{2A}{b}$$.

blocks.title: Circle: Radius, Diameter, Area, Circumference
blocks.content:
For circles, first check whether the problem gives radius or diameter.

Radius = center to edge.

Diameter = across the whole circle.

$$d=2r$$

Circle area means space inside:

$$A=\pi r^2$$

Circle circumference means distance around:

$$C=2\pi r$$

Tiny teaching example:
Radius = 7.

Area:

$$A=\pi(7^2)=49\pi$$

Circumference:

$$C=2\pi(7)=14\pi$$

Teacher note:
Area squares the radius. Circumference does not.

blocks.title: Practice — Clock Hand / Fraction of Circle
blocks.content:
Problem:
The hour hand of a clock is 3 feet long. How many feet does the tip travel from 1300 to 1700?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: The tip of the hand moves around a circle.

Radius = 3 feet.

Step 2: Find the full circumference.

$$C=2\pi r=2\pi(3)=6\pi$$

Step 3: Find the fraction of the circle.

From 1300 to 1700 is 4 hours.

A full clock rotation is 12 hours.

$$\frac{4}{12}=\frac{1}{3}$$

Step 4: Take 1/3 of the circumference.

$$\frac{1}{3}(6\pi)=2\pi$$

Answer: $$2\pi$$ feet.

Teacher note:
This is just a fraction of circumference problem.

blocks.title: Right Triangles: Pythagorean Theorem
blocks.content:
For a right triangle:

$$a^2+b^2=c^2$$

$c$ is the hypotenuse, which is the longest side across from the right angle.

Common triples to recognize:

$$3,4,5$$

$$5,12,13$$

$$8,15,17$$

$$7,24,25$$

Multiples also work:

$$6,8,10$$

$$10,24,26$$

Teacher note:
Recognizing triples saves time because you do not need to calculate the square root.

blocks.title: Practice — Rectangle Diagonal
blocks.content:
Problem:
A rectangle has length 8 and width 6. What is the diagonal?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: The diagonal makes a right triangle.

The legs are 8 and 6.

Step 2: Use the Pythagorean theorem.

$$d^2=8^2+6^2$$

$$d^2=64+36=100$$

$$d=10$$

Answer: 10.

Teacher note:
This is the 6-8-10 triple.

blocks.title: Special Right Triangles
blocks.content:
Two special triangles are worth memorizing.

45-45-90 triangle:

$$x,x,x\sqrt{2}$$

This means both legs are equal, and the hypotenuse is leg times $\sqrt{2}$.

30-60-90 triangle:

$$x,x\sqrt{3},2x$$

This means:

short leg = x

long leg = x√3

hypotenuse = 2x

Teacher note:
For 30-60-90, the shortest side is across from 30°. The hypotenuse is double the short leg.

blocks.title: Practice — 30-60-90 Triangle
blocks.content:
Problem:
In a 30-60-90 triangle, the hypotenuse is 10. What are the two legs?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Use the ratio.

30-60-90:

$$x,x\sqrt{3},2x$$

Step 2: The hypotenuse is 2x.

$$2x=10$$

$$x=5$$

Step 3: Find the legs.

Short leg = 5.

Long leg = $$5\sqrt{3}$$.

Answer: short leg = 5, long leg = $$5\sqrt{3}$$.

Teacher note:
Do not confuse this with 45-45-90, where the legs are equal.

blocks.title: 3D Volume: Rectangular Prism and Cylinder
blocks.content:
Volume means space inside a 3D object.

Rectangular prism:

$$V=lwh$$

Cylinder:

$$V=\pi r^2h$$

Tiny teaching example:
A cylinder has radius 3 and height 7.

$$V=\pi(3^2)(7)=63\pi$$

Teacher note:
For a cylinder, square the radius first. Do not use diameter unless you divide by 2 first.

blocks.title: Practice — Empty Space: Box Minus Cylinder
blocks.content:
Problem:
A cylinder with radius 3 and height 5 is placed inside a rectangular box with a 6×6 square base and height 5. What is the volume of empty space?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Empty space means subtract.

Empty space = box volume − cylinder volume.

Step 2: Find box volume.

$$V=lwh=6(6)(5)=180$$

Step 3: Find cylinder volume.

$$V=\pi r^2h=\pi(3^2)(5)=45\pi$$

Step 4: Subtract.

Empty space:

$$180-45\pi$$

Answer: $$180-45\pi$$.

Teacher note:
For inside/outside space problems, find the big shape first, then subtract the smaller shape.

blocks.title: Radians, Arc Length, and Sector Area
blocks.content:
Some circle problems use radians.

Radian-to-degree conversion:

$$\text{degrees}=\text{radians}\times\frac{180}{\pi}$$

Arc length is distance along the edge of a circle:

$$s=\theta r$$

Sector area is area of a slice of a circle:

$$A=\frac{1}{2}r^2\theta$$

Important:
$\theta$ must be in radians for these formulas.

Teacher note:
Arc length is edge distance. Sector area is slice area.

blocks.title: Practice — Arc Length
blocks.content:
Problem:
A sector has central angle $\pi/3$ radians and radius 6. What is the arc length?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Use arc length formula.

$$s=\theta r$$

Step 2: Substitute.

$$s=\frac{\pi}{3}(6)$$

$$s=2\pi$$

Answer: $$2\pi$$.

Teacher note:
This works directly because the angle is already in radians.

blocks.title: Ratios and Similar Shapes
blocks.content:
Some geometry problems ask how a shape changes when one measurement changes.

Key idea:

Length changes by the scale factor.

Area changes by the scale factor squared.

Volume changes by the scale factor cubed, unless only one measurement changes.

Cylinder with same height:

$$V=\pi r^2h$$

If height stays the same, volume changes with $r^2$.

Teacher note:
If radius changes from 3 to 5 and height stays the same, compare $3^2$ to $5^2$, not 3 to 5.

blocks.title: Sphere Formulas: Know, But Lower Priority
blocks.content:
Sphere questions can ask surface area or volume.

Surface area:

$$SA=4\pi r^2$$

Volume:

$$V=\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$$

These are harder to remember, and some practice questions may give them directly in the problem.

Teacher note:
If you have limited scratch-paper space, memorize circle/cylinder/right-triangle formulas before sphere formulas.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Geometry
blocks.content:
Write this as a categorized scratch-paper guide.

RECTANGLE

Area:

$$A=lw$$

Perimeter:

$$P=2l+2w$$

TRIANGLE

Area:

$$A=\frac{1}{2}bh$$

RIGHT TRIANGLE

Pythagorean theorem:

$$a^2+b^2=c^2$$

Triples:

$$3,4,5$$

$$5,12,13$$

$$8,15,17$$

SPECIAL TRIANGLES

45-45-90:

$$x,x,x\sqrt{2}$$

30-60-90:

$$x,x\sqrt{3},2x$$

CIRCLE

Area:

$$A=\pi r^2$$

Circumference:

$$C=2\pi r$$

Diameter:

$$d=2r$$

VOLUME

Rectangular prism:

$$V=lwh$$

Cylinder:

$$V=\pi r^2h$$

ARC / SECTOR

Degrees from radians:

$$\text{degrees}=\text{radians}\times\frac{180}{\pi}$$

Arc length:

$$s=\theta r$$

Sector area:

$$A=\frac{1}{2}r^2\theta$$

Lower priority if space is limited:

Sphere surface area:

$$SA=4\pi r^2$$

Sphere volume:

$$V=\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$$

Teacher note:
If you only have space for a few formulas, prioritize triangle, rectangle, circle, Pythagorean theorem, special triangles, and cylinder volume.

blocks.title: Quick Patch: Complementary vs. Supplementary Angles
blocks.content:
This was missing from the original lesson, and it is important for OAR-style geometry.

Complementary angles add up to 90°.

$$A+B=90^\circ$$

Supplementary angles add up to 180°.

$$A+B=180^\circ$$

Memory trick:

Complementary = corner = 90° angle.

Supplementary = straight line = 180° angle.

Tiny teaching example:
If one angle is 35° and it asks for its complement:

$$90-35=55^\circ$$

If one angle is 35° and it asks for its supplement:

$$180-35=145^\circ$$

Teacher note:
This is exactly the kind of tiny definition mistake that can ruin an easy question. When you see complement, think 90. When you see supplement, think 180.

blocks.title: Practice — Angle Is Smaller Than Its Complement
blocks.content:
Problem:
Angle F is 30° smaller than its complement. What is the number of degrees in angle F?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: Know the definition.

An angle and its complement add to 90°.

Step 2: Translate the words.

Angle F is 30° smaller than its complement.

That means the complement is 30° bigger than F.

So:

$$F+(F+30)=90$$

Step 3: Solve.

$$2F+30=90$$

$$2F=60$$

$$F=30$$

Answer: 30°.

Check:
The complement of 30° is 60°.
30° is 30° smaller than 60°, so it works.

Teacher note:
If the wording says 'Angle F is 30 smaller than its complement,' do NOT write F + 30 = 90. That would only find the complement of a known angle. Here, both angles are unknown, so use F and F+30.

blocks.title: Quick Patch: Linear Pair and Vertical Angles
blocks.content:
Two more angle facts can show up in geometry diagrams.

Linear pair:
Two angles form a straight line, so they add to 180°.

$$A+B=180^\circ$$

Vertical angles:
Opposite angles formed by two crossing lines are equal.

Example:
If two lines cross and one angle is 72°, the angle directly across from it is also 72°.

Teacher note:
A straight line is 180°. A corner/right angle is 90°. Opposite crossing angles are equal.

blocks.title: Quick Patch: Circle Central Angles and Isosceles Radii
blocks.content:
In a circle, any segment from the center to the edge is a radius.

All radii in the same circle are equal.

This means if two radii form a triangle with a chord, the triangle is isosceles.

Isosceles triangle:
Two sides are equal, so the two base angles are equal.

Triangle angles always add to 180°.

$$A+B+C=180^\circ$$

Central angle:
An angle with its vertex at the center of the circle.

If a central angle is 80°, then the remaining two base angles in the isosceles triangle split the leftover angle equally.

$$180-80=100$$

$$100\div2=50$$

Teacher note:
For circle diagrams with two radii, look for an isosceles triangle hiding inside the circle.

blocks.title: Practice — Central Angle in a Circle
blocks.content:
Problem:
In a circle, OA and OB are radii. The central angle AOB is 70°. What is the measure of angle OAB?
blocks.solution:
Step 1: OA and OB are both radii.

So OA = OB.

That makes triangle AOB isosceles.

Step 2: Triangle angles add to 180°.

$$180-70=110$$

Step 3: The two base angles are equal, so divide by 2.

$$110\div2=55$$

Answer: 55°.

Teacher note:
Do not subtract from 90 here. This is not a complementary angle problem. It is a triangle angle-sum problem.

blocks.title: Quick Patch: Similar Triangles / Shadow Problems
blocks.content:
Shadow problems usually use similar triangles.

If two objects stand upright and the sun hits them at the same angle, their height-to-shadow ratios match.

$$\frac{\text{small height}}{\text{small shadow}}=\frac{\text{large height}}{\text{large shadow}}$$

Tiny teaching example:
A 6 ft person casts a 10 ft shadow. A pole casts a 40 ft shadow. Find the pole height.

$$\frac{6}{10}=\frac{x}{40}$$

$$10x=240$$

$$x=24$$

Answer: 24 ft.

Teacher note:
Match height with height and shadow with shadow. Do not mix height with shadow diagonally.

blocks.title: Quick Patch: Cube Edge Sum vs. Volume
blocks.content:
A cube has 12 equal edges.

If the sum of all cube edges is given, divide by 12 to find one side length.

$$s=\frac{\text{sum of edges}}{12}$$

Cube volume:

$$V=s^3$$

Tiny teaching example:
The sum of all edges of a cube is 36 inches. What is the volume?

$$s=36\div12=3$$

$$V=3^3=27$$

Answer: 27 cubic inches.

Teacher note:
Do not confuse edges with faces. A cube has 12 edges and 6 faces.

blocks.title: Updated Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Geometry Add-On
blocks.content:
Add these missing angle and diagram facts under your existing geometry scratch-paper formulas.

ANGLES

Complementary:

$$A+B=90^\circ$$

Supplementary / straight line / linear pair:

$$A+B=180^\circ$$

Triangle angle sum:

$$A+B+C=180^\circ$$

Vertical angles:

Opposite angles are equal.

CIRCLE DIAGRAMS

All radii in the same circle are equal.

Two radii often create an isosceles triangle.

For an isosceles triangle, the two base angles are equal.

SIMILAR TRIANGLES / SHADOWS

$$\frac{\text{height}_1}{\text{shadow}_1}=\frac{\text{height}_2}{\text{shadow}_2}$$

CUBE

A cube has 12 equal edges.

$$s=\frac{\text{total edge length}}{12}$$

$$V=s^3$$

Teacher note:
For your next test, the most important new facts are complement = 90°, supplement = 180°, and triangle sum = 180°.

key_formulas: ARC LENGTH, θ in radians: $$s=\theta r$$
SPHERE — surface area, lower priority: $$SA=4\pi r^2$$
CIRCLE DIAGRAMS — all radii in the same circle are equal; two radii create an isosceles triangle
CUBE — 12 equal edges; total edge length $$=12s$$; volume $$V=s^3$$
common_mistakes: Using diameter as radius.
Forgetting to square the radius in circle area or cylinder volume.
Using perimeter when the question asks area.
Using area when the question asks perimeter.
Forgetting the hypotenuse is the longest side.
Forgetting that arc length and sector formulas require radians.
Not drawing the shape before solving.
For inside/outside area or volume problems, forgetting to subtract the smaller shape from the larger shape.
Confusing complementary angles with supplementary angles.
Writing F + 30 = 90 when the problem says an angle is 30 degrees smaller than its complement.
Forgetting that triangle angles add to 180 degrees.
Not noticing that two radii form an isosceles triangle in a circle diagram.
Mixing up height and shadow when setting up similar-triangle proportions.

conceptPages.title: Arc Length & Sectors
conceptPages.overview: Arc length and sector problems are circle-slice problems. The key is to identify whether the question asks for curved edge length, slice area, or full boundary/perimeter. Then check whether the angle is in radians or degrees.

blocks.title: Start Here: A Sector Is a Pizza Slice
blocks.content:
A sector is a slice of a circle.

It has:

1. radius

2. central angle

3. arc length around the curved edge

4. sector area inside the slice

OAR-style questions usually ask for arc length, sector area, or the distance traveled by the tip of a clock hand.

blocks.title: Radians Formulas
blocks.content:
If the angle is in radians:

Arc length:
$$s=r\theta$$

Sector area:
$$A=\frac{1}{2}r^2\theta$$

Here $\theta$ is the angle in radians.

These formulas are fast, but only use them directly when the angle is in radians.

blocks.title: Degrees Method
blocks.content:
If the angle is in degrees, use the fraction of the full circle.

Full circle is 360 degrees.

Arc length:
$$s=\frac{\theta}{360}\times 2\pi r$$

Sector area:
$$A=\frac{\theta}{360}\times \pi r^2$$

Teacher shortcut:
Degrees means fraction of circle.
Radians means use $s=r\theta$ and $A=\frac{1}{2}r^2\theta$.

blocks.title: Example 1 — Arc Length in Radians
blocks.content:
Problem: A circle has radius 6 and central angle $\pi/3$ radians. Find the arc length.

Use:
$$s=r\theta$$

Plug in:
$$s=6\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=2\pi$$

Answer: $2\pi$.

blocks.title: Example 2 — Sector Area in Degrees
blocks.content:
Problem: Find the sector area for radius 5 and central angle 210 degrees.

Use the degree formula:
$$A=\frac{210}{360}\pi(5^2)$$

Simplify the fraction:
$$\frac{210}{360}=\frac{7}{12}$$

So:
$$A=\frac{7}{12}\times 25\pi=\frac{175\pi}{12}$$

Answer: $175\pi/12$.

blocks.title: Example 3 — Find Radius From Arc and Area
blocks.content:
Problem: A sector has arc length $10\pi$ and sector area $30\pi$. Find the radius.

Use the relationship:
$$s=r\theta$$
$$A=\frac{1}{2}r^2\theta$$

Since $s=r\theta$, substitute into area:
$$A=\frac{1}{2}r(r\theta)=\frac{1}{2}rs$$

So:
$$30\pi=\frac{1}{2}r(10\pi)$$
$$30\pi=5\pi r$$
$$r=6$$

Answer: 6.

blocks.title: Example 4 — Clock Hand Distance
blocks.content:
Problem: The hour hand of a clock is 4 feet long. How far does the tip travel from 8:00 to 2:00?

From 8 to 2 is 6 hours.

The hour hand makes half a circle in 6 hours.

Full circumference:
$$2\pi r=2\pi(4)=8\pi$$

Half of that:
$$4\pi$$

Answer: $4\pi$ feet.

blocks.title: Patch 1 — What the Symbols Mean
blocks.content:
In this lesson:

$r$ means radius.

$\theta$ means the central angle.

$s$ means arc length, which is the curved edge of the sector.

$A$ means sector area, which is the space inside the slice.

Teacher note:
If the question asks for distance traveled by a clock hand, wheel rim, or point on a circle, that is usually arc length.

If the question asks for shaded region or slice area, that is sector area.

blocks.title: Patch 2 — Same Idea, Two Ways
blocks.content:
There are two safe ways to solve these problems.

Method 1: Radian formulas

Use when the angle is already in radians:

$$s=r\theta$$

$$A=\frac{1}{2}r^2\theta$$

Method 2: Fraction of a circle

Use when the angle is in degrees, or when the angle is an easy part of a circle:

$$\text{fraction}=\frac{\theta}{360}$$

Then:

$$\text{arc length}=\text{fraction}\times 2\pi r$$

$$\text{sector area}=\text{fraction}\times \pi r^2$$

Teacher note:
Both methods give the same answer. Use whichever feels safer.

blocks.title: Patch 3 — Common Circle Fractions
blocks.content:
These are worth recognizing so you do not overwork the problem.

$$30^\circ=\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{1}{12}\text{ of a circle}$$

$$45^\circ=\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{1}{8}\text{ of a circle}$$

$$60^\circ=\frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{1}{6}\text{ of a circle}$$

$$90^\circ=\frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{1}{4}\text{ of a circle}$$

$$120^\circ=\frac{2\pi}{3}=\frac{1}{3}\text{ of a circle}$$

$$180^\circ=\pi=\frac{1}{2}\text{ of a circle}$$

Teacher note:
If the angle is $\pi/2$, it is a quarter circle. If the angle is $\pi$, it is a semicircle.

blocks.title: Patch 4 — Sector Perimeter
blocks.content:
Problem:
A sector has radius 6 and angle $\pi/3$. What is the perimeter of the sector?

Step 1: Find the arc length.

$$s=r\theta=6\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=2\pi$$

Step 2: Add the two straight sides.

A sector has two radii, so add $2r$.

$$P=s+2r$$

$$P=2\pi+2(6)=2\pi+12$$

Answer: $12+2\pi$.

Teacher note:
Perimeter means the whole outside boundary: curved edge plus two radii. Do not stop after finding only the arc.

blocks.title: Patch 5 — Solving Backward
blocks.content:
Sometimes the question gives the arc length or sector area and asks for radius or angle.

From arc length:

$$s=r\theta$$

Solve for radius:

$$r=\frac{s}{\theta}$$

Solve for angle:

$$\theta=\frac{s}{r}$$

From sector area:

$$A=\frac{1}{2}r^2\theta$$

Solve for angle:

$$\theta=\frac{2A}{r^2}$$

If the problem gives both arc length and sector area, use this shortcut:

$$r=\frac{2A}{s}$$

Teacher note:
Do not memorize every rearranged formula if it feels overwhelming. You can start from the original formula and isolate the missing letter.

blocks.title: Scratch Paper Formula Flow — Arc Length & Sectors
blocks.content:
1. Write the circle basics.

$$C=2\pi r$$

$$A_{circle}=\pi r^2$$

2. Ask what the problem wants.

Curved edge distance → arc length.

Slice/shaded space → sector area.

Whole outside boundary of slice → sector perimeter.

3. If angle is in radians:

$$s=r\theta$$

$$A=\frac{1}{2}r^2\theta$$

4. If angle is in degrees:

$$\text{fraction}=\frac{\theta}{360}$$

$$s=\frac{\theta}{360}(2\pi r)$$

$$A=\frac{\theta}{360}(\pi r^2)$$

5. Sector perimeter:

$$P=s+2r$$

6. Backward shortcut if both arc length and sector area are given:

$$r=\frac{2A}{s}$$

key_formulas: $$r=\frac{2A}{s} \quad \text{when arc length and sector area are both given}$$
common_mistakes: Using radian formulas when the angle is given in degrees without converting.
Forgetting that a clock hand travels along an arc, not a straight line.
Using diameter instead of radius.
Forgetting to square the radius in sector area.
Finding only the arc length when the question asks for sector perimeter.
Mixing up arc length and sector area: arc length has regular units, sector area has square units.
